## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Time-Domain Governing Equations of a Linear Transformer

Let coil-1 (port-1) carry current $i_1(t)$ and coil-2 (port-2) carry current $i_2(t)$. The fluxes linking each coil are $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$:

$$\lambda_1(t) = L_1 i_1(t) \pm M i_2(t)$$
$$\lambda_2(t) = L_2 i_2(t) \pm M i_1(t)$$

Applying Faraday's law ($v = d\lambda/dt$) and including series resistance $R_1, R_2$:

$$v_1(t) = R_1 i_1(t) + L_1 \frac{di_1}{dt} \pm M \frac{di_2}{dt}$$
$$v_2(t) = R_2 i_2(t) + L_2 \frac{di_2}{dt} \pm M \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

The **dot convention** dictates the sign of $M$:
- If both currents enter the dotted terminals (or both leave) → **positive** sign
- If one enters dotted and the other leaves dotted → **negative** sign

### 2.2 Transformation to the s-Domain

With **zero initial conditions**, applying the unilateral Laplace transform ($\mathcal{L}\{di/dt\} = sI(s)$) yields:

$$V_1(s) = R_1 I_1(s) + s L_1 I_1(s) \pm s M I_2(s)$$
$$V_2(s) = R_2 I_2(s) + s L_2 I_2(s) \pm s M I_1(s)$$

Collecting terms:

$$\boxed{V_1(s) = (R_1 + sL_1) I_1(s) \pm sM \cdot I_2(s)}$$
$$\boxed{V_2(s) = \pm sM \cdot I_1(s) + (R_2 + sL_2) I_2(s)}$$

### 2.3 The Z-Parameter (Open-Circuit Impedance) Representation

Comparing the s-domain equations with the standard two-port definition $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$, we identify:

$$Z_{11}(s) = R_1 + sL_1, \quad Z_{22}(s) = R_2 + sL_2, \quad Z_{12}(s) = Z_{21}(s) = \pm sM$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1(s) \\ V_2(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_1 + sL_1 & \pm sM \\ \pm sM & R_2 + sL_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1(s) \\ I_2(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Property:** A linear transformer is a *reciprocal* two-port because $Z_{12}(s) = Z_{21}(s)$. The s-domain Z-matrix is **symmetric** about the principal diagonal — a favourite KTU short-answer question!

### 2.4 Input Impedance & Reflected Impedance (Loaded Case)

When a load $Z_L(s)$ is connected at port-2 ($V_2 = -Z_L I_2$), the input impedance seen at port-1 is:

$$Z_{in}(s) = \frac{V_1(s)}{I_1(s)} = Z_{11}(s) - \frac{Z_{12}(s) Z_{21}(s)}{Z_{22}(s) + Z_L(s)}$$

Substituting the coupled-circuit values:

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(s) = (R_1 + sL_1) - \frac{(\pm sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)} = (R_1 + sL_1) + Z_{reflected}(s)}$$

The **reflected impedance** is therefore:

$$Z_{reflected}(s) = -\frac{(sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *negative* sign indicates the reflected impedance is subtracted from the primary self-impedance — this is the electrical manifestation of the *magnetic loading* caused by the secondary current.

### 2.5 The T-Equivalent (T-Net) Circuit

A coupled circuit with **no actual magnetic coupling** can be drawn as a three-branch T-network using three uncoupled inductors, provided the *dot convention is respected*:

| Branch Position | Inductance Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Series branch (port-1 side) | $L_1 - M$ | $L_a$ |
| Shunt (middle) branch | $\pm M$ | $L_b$ |
| Series branch (port-2 side) | $L_2 - M$ | $L_c$ |

The T-equivalent is essential for analysing coupled circuits using **standard nodal/mesh analysis tools** that don't natively support mutual coupling.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula (s-Domain) | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{11}(s) = R_1 + sL_1$ | Primary self-impedance | Open-circuit test at port-1 |
| 2 | $Z_{22}(s) = R_2 + sL_2$ | Secondary self-impedance | Open-circuit test at port-2 |
| 3 | $Z_{12}(s) = Z_{21}(s) = \pm sM$ | Coupling impedance (s-domain) | Dot convention application |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | Coupling coefficient, $0 \le k \le 1$ | Determine coupling quality |
| 5 | $Z_{in}(s) = Z_{11} - \dfrac{(sM)^2}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$ | Driving-point impedance | Solve for primary current |
| 6 | $Z_r(s) = -\dfrac{(sM)^2}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$ | Reflected impedance | Impedance transformation ratio |
| 7 | $H(s) = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{sM \cdot Z_L}{Z_{11}(Z_{22}+Z_L)-(sM)^2}$ | Voltage transfer ratio | Filter design, transformer gain |
| 8 | T-equivalent: $L_a = L_1 - M$, $L_b = M$, $L_c = L_2 - M$ | Inductor-only T-network | Mesh/nodal analysis conversion |
| 9 | $\Delta Z(s) = Z_{11}Z_{22} - (sM)^2$ | Determinant of Z-matrix | Z-parameter analysis |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Transformers & Isolation Amplifiers:** The s-domain model predicts frequency response and isolation bandwidth.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** $Z_{in}$ governs the inrush current and snubber design.
- **RF Baluns & Impedance Matching Networks:** The reflected impedance $Z_r$ is the core design tool for matching antenna impedance to transmission-line impedance (e.g., $50\Omega$).
- **EMI/EMC Filters:** Coupled inductors in EMI filters are designed using exactly these s-domain equations to create common-mode chokes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Input Impedance in the s-Domain

**Given:** A two-winding linear transformer with $R_1, L_1, R_2, L_2, M$ and a load $Z_L(s)$ at port-2. Both currents entering the dotted terminals (positive dot convention).

**Step 1 — Write the s-domain mesh equations** using the standard coupled-coil voltage law with the load relationship $V_2(s) = -Z_L(s) I_2(s)$:

$$V_1(s) = (R_1 + sL_1) I_1(s) + sM \cdot I_2(s) \quad \text{...(Eq. M1)}$$
$$0 = sM \cdot I_1(s) + (R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L) I_2(s) \quad \text{...(Eq. M2)}$$

**Step 2 — Express $I_2$ in terms of $I_1$ from Eq. M2** by isolating $I_2$:

$$I_2(s) = -\frac{sM \cdot I_1(s)}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)}$$

*Conversion logic:* Rearrange the second equation so $I_2$ is the subject. The $V_2 = -Z_L I_2$ sign convention (passive sign convention for load) is what produces the negative sign.

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $I_2$ into Eq. M1** to eliminate $I_2$:

$$V_1(s) = (R_1 + sL_1) I_1(s) + sM \cdot \left( -\frac{sM \cdot I_1(s)}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $I_1(s)$ from the right-hand side**:

$$V_1(s) = I_1(s) \left[ (R_1 + sL_1) - \frac{(sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Define the input impedance as the ratio $V_1(s) / I_1(s)$**:

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(s) = (R_1 + sL_1) - \frac{(sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the reflected impedance term**:

$$Z_{reflected}(s) = -\frac{(sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)} = -\frac{\omega^2 M^2}{R_2 + j\omega L_2 + Z_L} \bigg\vert_{s = j\omega}$$

*Conversion logic:* The negative sign on $Z_{reflected}$ is a direct consequence of the Lenz-law opposition — the secondary current produces a flux that *opposes* the cause that produced it, appearing as a *subtractive* term in the primary impedance.

**Step 7 — Solve for the primary current** when source $V_1(s)$ is given:

$$I_1(s) = \frac{V_1(s)}{Z_{in}(s)}$$

**Step 8 — Find the secondary current** by back-substituting $I_1$ into the Step 2 expression:

$$I_2(s) = -\frac{sM}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s)} \cdot I_1(s) = -\frac{sM \cdot V_1(s)}{Z_{in}(s) \left[ R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s) \right]}$$

**Step 9 — Find the output voltage across the load**:

$$V_{out}(s) = -Z_L(s) \cdot I_2(s) = \frac{sM \cdot Z_L(s) \cdot V_1(s)}{Z_{in}(s) \left[ R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L(s) \right]}$$

**Step 10 — The voltage transfer function** in its most compact form:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_1(s)} = \frac{sM \cdot Z_L(s)}{(R_1 + sL_1)(R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L) - (sM)^2}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the T-Equivalent Circuit

**Step 1 — Start from the s-domain voltage equations** (positive dot convention):

$$V_1 = (R_1 + sL_1) I_1 + sM \cdot I_2$$
$$V_2 = sM \cdot I_1 + (R_2 + sL_2) I_2$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $sM \cdot I_1$ and $sM \cdot I_2$ strategically** to introduce new variables:

$$V_1 = (R_1 + sL_1 - sM) I_1 + sM(I_1 + I_2)$$
$$V_2 = sM(I_1 + I_2) + (R_2 + sL_2 - sM) I_2$$

**Step 3 — Define the mesh currents of the T-equivalent**. Let $I_3 = I_1 + I_2$ be the current through the *shunt* branch (middle leg). Then:

$$V_1 = (R_1 + sL_1 - sM) I_1 + sM \cdot I_3$$
$$V_2 = sM \cdot I_3 + (R_2 + sL_2 - sM) I_2$$

**Step 4 — Read the branch impedances of the T-equivalent** directly:

- Left series branch: $Z_a(s) = R_1 + s(L_1 - M)$
- Middle shunt branch: $Z_b(s) = sM$
- Right series branch: $Z_c(s) = R_2 + s(L_2 - M)$

**Step 5 — Verify the equivalence** by computing the Z-matrix of the T-network and confirming it equals the original coupled-coil Z-matrix. This completes the proof.

> [!NOTE]
> The T-equivalent is only **purely inductive** (i.e., $L_a, L_b, L_c \ge 0$) when the dot convention is consistent. If $M > L_1$ or $M > L_2$ (rare, indicates over-coupling in sign), the equivalent inductances may be negative — signalling a sign flip in the dot convention.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solved Numerical Example

**Problem:** A linear transformer has $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $L_1 = 3\,\text{H}$, $R_2 = 4\,\Omega$, $L_2 = 5\,\text{H}$, $M = 2\,\text{H}$, and is terminated in a load $Z_L = 10\,\Omega$. The source is $V_1(s) = \dfrac{100}{s}$ V (a 100 V step input). Find $I_1(s), I_2(s)$ and the steady-state primary current at $t \to \infty$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Z-matrix elements**:

$$Z_{11} = 2 + 3s, \quad Z_{22} = 4 + 5s, \quad Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 2s$$

**Step 2 — Form the mesh equations**:

$$V_1(s) = (2 + 3s) I_1 + 2s \cdot I_2$$
$$0 = 2s \cdot I_1 + (4 + 5s + 10) I_2 = 2s \cdot I_1 + (14 + 5s) I_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system**. Use Cramer's rule. The determinant:

$$\Delta = (2 + 3s)(14 + 5s) - (2s)^2 = 28 + 10s + 42s + 15s^2 - 4s^2 = 11s^2 + 52s + 28$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta_1$** (replace first column with $[V_1(s), 0]^T$):

$$\Delta_1 = V_1(s) \cdot (14 + 5s) = \frac{100(14 + 5s)}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Delta_2$**:

$$\Delta_2 = V_1(s) \cdot (-2s) = -\frac{200s}{s} = -200$$

**Step 6 — Primary current**:

$$I_1(s) = \frac{\Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{100(14 + 5s)}{s(11s^2 + 52s + 28)}$$

**Step 7 — Secondary current**:

$$I_2(s) = \frac{\Delta_2}{\Delta} = \frac{-200}{11s^2 + 52s + 28}$$

**Step 8 — Steady-state (Final Value Theorem)** for $I_1$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} i_1(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot I_1(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{100(14 + 5s)}{11s^2 + 52s + 28} = \frac{100 \times 14}{28} = 50\,\text{A}$$

**Step 9 — Steady-state secondary current**:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} i_2(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot I_2(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{-200 s}{11s^2 + 52s + 28} = 0\,\text{A}$$

*Engineering interpretation:* At $t \to \infty$, the inductors behave as short-circuits, so the primary sees only $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$ (DC steady state of an inductor). The current divides, and with both ends shorted via $R_2 + Z_L = 14\,\Omega$, the secondary current is non-zero momentarily but in *true DC steady state* with a step input it goes to 0 — wait, let me correct this:

Actually for **DC steady state** ($s \to 0$): Inductors act as short circuits, so $Z_{in}(0) = R_1 - \frac{0}{R_2 + Z_L} = R_1 = 2\,\Omega$. Thus $I_1(\infty) = V_1/R_1 = 100/2 = 50\,\text{A}$. The secondary current $I_2 = -\frac{0 \cdot 100}{...} = 0$ because there is no rate-of-change of current in steady state, hence no induced EMF in the secondary.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
*Define mutual inductance and coupling coefficient for a linear transformer. What is the range of the coupling coefficient?*

**Model Answer:**
Mutual inductance $M$ between two coils is defined as the flux linkage in one coil per unit current in the other coil, with units of **Henry (H)**. It quantifies the magnetic coupling between the two windings.

The coupling coefficient $k$ is the fraction of total flux produced by one coil that links the other coil:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}, \quad 0 \le k \le 1$$

- $k = 0$: No coupling (magnetically isolated coils)
- $k = 1$: Perfect coupling (all flux from one coil links the other)
- $0 < k < 1$: Partial coupling (typical for real transformers)

A *linear* transformer obeys $k = \text{constant}$ independent of current magnitude, which is the foundational assumption enabling the s-domain linear analysis.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Write the s-domain Z-parameter equations of a two-winding linear transformer with self-inductances $L_1, L_2$, mutual inductance $M$, and winding resistances $R_1, R_2$. Mention the reciprocity property.*

**Model Answer:**
The s-domain Z-parameter representation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1(s) \\ V_2(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_1 + sL_1 & sM \\ sM & R_2 + sL_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1(s) \\ I_2(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

(Assuming positive dot convention.)

**Reciprocity Property:** The Z-matrix is symmetric, i.e., $Z_{12}(s) = Z_{21}(s) = sM$. This is a direct consequence of the linear, passive, and bilateral nature of the magnetic coupling — the voltage induced in coil-2 by current in coil-1 equals the voltage induced in coil-1 by the same current in coil-2.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $sM$:** Many students blindly write $+sM$ in both off-diagonal positions. If the *dot convention* is violated (e.g., one current enters dot, the other leaves), the sign **flips**. Always re-read the dot markings before writing equations.
> 2. **Reflected Impedance Sign:** The reflected impedance is **subtracted** from $Z_{11}$ (not added). Forgetting the negative sign yields a wrong $Z_{in}$ and propagates an error to every downstream current/voltage.
> 3. **Units:** $s$ has units of **rad/s** (or $\text{s}^{-1}$), and $sL$ has units of ohms. Mixing $M$ in **milli-Henrys** with $L$ in **Henrys** is a 1-mark cut in valuation.
> 4. **T-Equivalent $L_1 - M$ vs $L_1 + M$:** A positive dot convention uses $-M$ in the series arms. A negative dot convention uses $+M$ in the series arms. Many students hard-code one form — avoid this trap.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the coupled circuit shown, derive the s-domain expression for input impedance $Z_{in}(s)$ seen at port-1 when port-2 is terminated in a load $Z_L(s)$. Clearly identify the reflected impedance term.

*(Assume $R_1 = 1\,\Omega$, $L_1 = 2\,\text{H}$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, $L_2 = 4\,\text{H}$, $M = 1\,\text{H}$, $Z_L = 5\,\Omega$ — compute $Z_{in}(s)$ as a rational function in $s$.)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Form the s-domain equations with the load boundary condition $V_2 = -Z_L I_2$:

$$V_1 = (1 + 2s) I_1 + s \cdot I_2$$
$$0 = s \cdot I_1 + (3 + 4s + 5) I_2 = s \cdot I_1 + (8 + 4s) I_2$$

**Step 2:** From equation 2:

$$I_2 = -\frac{s \, I_1}{8 + 4s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into equation 1:

$$V_1 = (1 + 2s) I_1 + s \cdot \left( -\frac{s I_1}{8 + 4s} \right) = I_1 \left[ (1 + 2s) - \frac{s^2}{8 + 4s} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Common denominator $(8 + 4s)$:

$$V_1 = I_1 \cdot \frac{(1 + 2s)(8 + 4s) - s^2}{8 + 4s}$$

**Step 5:** Expand the numerator:

$$(1 + 2s)(8 + 4s) - s^2 = 8 + 4s + 16s + 8s^2 - s^2 = 8 + 20s + 7s^2$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(s) = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{7s^2 + 20s + 8}{4s + 8} = \frac{7s^2 + 20s + 8}{4(s + 2)}\,\Omega}$$

**Reflected impedance identification:**

$$Z_{reflected}(s) = -\frac{(sM)^2}{R_2 + sL_2 + Z_L} = -\frac{s^2}{8 + 4s} = -\frac{s^2}{4(s+2)}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same circuit, find the voltage transfer function $H(s) = V_{out}(s) / V_1(s)$ and evaluate $|H(j\omega)|$ at $\omega = 1\,\text{rad/s}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The output voltage is $V_{out} = -Z_L \cdot I_2$ (passive sign convention). Using $I_2$ from Step 2 of part (a):

$$I_2 = -\frac{s \cdot I_1}{8 + 4s}$$

**Step 2:** And from the input impedance relation $I_1 = V_1 / Z_{in}$:

$$I_2 = -\frac{s \cdot V_1}{Z_{in}(s) \cdot (8 + 4s)}$$

**Step 3:** The output voltage:

$$V_{out} = -Z_L \cdot I_2 = -5 \cdot \left( -\frac{s V_1}{Z_{in} (8 + 4s)} \right) = \frac{5s \cdot V_1}{Z_{in} (8 + 4s)}$$

**Step 4:** Transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1} = \frac{5s}{Z_{in}(s) \cdot (8 + 4s)} = \frac{5s \cdot 4(s+2)}{(7s^2 + 20s + 8)(8 + 4s)}$$

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{20s(s+2)}{(7s^2 + 20s + 8)(4s + 8)} = \frac{20s(s+2)}{4(7s^2 + 20s + 8)(s + 2)} = \frac{5s}{7s^2 + 20s + 8}}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at $s = j1$:

$$H(j1) = \frac{5j}{7(j)^2 + 20(j) + 8} = \frac{5j}{-7 + 20j + 8} = \frac{5j}{1 + 20j}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate $(1 - 20j)$:

$$H(j1) = \frac{5j(1 - 20j)}{1 + 400} = \frac{5j - 100j^2}{401} = \frac{5j + 100}{401} = \frac{100 + 5j}{401}$$

**Step 7:** Magnitude:

$$|H(j1)| = \frac{\sqrt{100^2 + 5^2}}{401} = \frac{\sqrt{10000 + 25}}{401} = \frac{\sqrt{10025}}{401} \approx \frac{100.125}{401} \approx 0.2497$$

$$\boxed{|H(j\omega = 1)| \approx 0.25 \text{ (dimensionless, V/V)}}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the T-equivalent circuit of a linear transformer with self-inductances $L_1 = 4\,\text{H}$, $L_2 = 9\,\text{H}$, mutual inductance $M = 3\,\text{H}$, and winding resistances $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\,\Omega$. Compute the branch impedances in the s-domain.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the T-equivalent branch components (positive dot convention):

| Branch | Inductance | Impedance in s-domain |
|---|---|---|
| Left series (port-1 side) | $L_1 - M = 4 - 3 = 1\,\text{H}$ | $Z_a = R_1 + s(L_1 - M) = 2 + s\,\Omega$ |
| Middle shunt | $M = 3\,\text{H}$ | $Z_b = sM = 3s\,\Omega$ |
| Right series (port-2 side) | $L_2 - M = 9 - 3 = 6\,\text{H}$ | $Z_c = R_2 + s(L_2 - M) = 5 + 6s\,\Omega$ |

**Step 2:** Final T-equivalent branch impedances:

$$\boxed{Z_a(s) = 2 + s, \quad Z_b(s) = 3s, \quad Z_c(s) = 5 + 6s \;\; \text{(all in ohms)}}$$

**Step 3:** Verification by computing the Z-matrix of the T-network and checking it matches the original coupled Z-matrix:

For a T-network, $Z_{11}^{T} = Z_a + Z_b = 2 + s + 3s = 2 + 4s$ ✓ (matches $R_1 + sL_1 = 2 + 4s$)

$Z_{22}^{T} = Z_c + Z_b = 5 + 6s + 3s = 5 + 9s$ ✓ (matches $R_2 + sL_2 = 5 + 9s$)

$Z_{12}^{T} = Z_{21}^{T} = Z_b = 3s$ ✓ (matches $sM = 3s$)

All three parameters match, confirming the T-equivalence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the T-equivalent, find the input impedance $Z_{in}(s)$ at port-1 when a load $Z_L = 10\,\Omega$ is connected at port-2. Also compute the input impedance at $\omega = 0$ (DC) and at $\omega = 10\,\text{rad/s}$ in the phasor domain.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For the T-network with load $Z_L$ at port-2, the right branch impedance is $Z_c + Z_L = 5 + 6s + 10 = 15 + 6s$.

**Step 2:** This combination is in series with $Z_b$ as a parallel combination? No — the T-network places $Z_b$ in shunt between the two series arms. The input impedance is $Z_a$ in series with the parallel combination of $Z_b$ and $(Z_c + Z_L)$:

$$Z_{in}(s) = Z_a + \frac{Z_b (Z_c + Z_L)}{Z_b + (Z_c + Z_L)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$Z_{in}(s) = (2 + s) + \frac{(3s)(15 + 6s)}{3s + 15 + 6s} = (2 + s) + \frac{3s(15 + 6s)}{9s + 15} = (2 + s) + \frac{s(15 + 6s)}{3s + 5}$$

**Step 4:** Common denominator $(3s + 5)$:

$$Z_{in}(s) = \frac{(2 + s)(3s + 5) + s(15 + 6s)}{3s + 5}$$

**Step 5:** Expand the numerator:

$$(2 + s)(3s + 5) = 6s + 10 + 3s^2 + 5s = 3s^2 + 11s + 10$$
$$s(15 + 6s) = 15s + 6s^2$$
$$\text{Sum} = 9s^2 + 26s + 10$$

**Step 6:**

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(s) = \frac{9s^2 + 26s + 10}{3s + 5}\,\Omega}$$

**Step 7:** DC evaluation ($\omega = 0 \Rightarrow s = 0$):

$$Z_{in}(0) = \frac{10}{5} = 2\,\Omega \quad \text{(equals } R_1 \text{ — as expected for DC shorted inductors)}$$

**Step 8:** Phasor evaluation at $\omega = 10\,\text{rad/s}$ ($s = j10$):

$$Z_{in}(j10) = \frac{9(j10)^2 + 26(j10) + 10}{3(j10) + 5} = \frac{-900 + 260j + 10}{5 + 30j} = \frac{-890 + 260j}{5 + 30j}$$

**Step 9:** Rationalise by multiplying by conjugate:

$$Z_{in}(j10) = \frac{(-890 + 260j)(5 - 30j)}{5^2 + 30^2} = \frac{-4450 + 26700j + 1300j - 7800j^2}{25 + 900}$$

$$= \frac{-4450 + 7800 + 28000j}{925} = \frac{3350 + 28000j}{925}$$

$$= 3.622 + 30.27j\,\Omega$$

**Step 10:** Magnitude and phase:

$$|Z_{in}(j10)| = \sqrt{3.622^2 + 30.27^2} \approx \sqrt{13.12 + 916.27} = \sqrt{929.39} \approx 30.49\,\Omega$$

$$\angle Z_{in}(j10) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{30.27}{3.622}\right) \approx 83.17°$$

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(j10) \approx 30.49 \angle 83.17°\,\Omega}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Coupling coefficient** $k = M/\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ is bounded between **0 and 1** (dimensionless).
- [x] The s-domain Z-matrix of a linear transformer is **symmetric**: $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = \pm sM$ → confirms **reciprocity**.
- [x] The sign of $sM$ is fixed by the **dot convention**: both currents into dots → positive; one in, one out → negative.
- [x] The **reflected impedance** is *subtracted* from $Z_{11}$, never added: $Z_r = -\dfrac{(sM)^2}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$.
- [x] **Input impedance** in s-domain: $Z_{in} = Z_{11} - \dfrac{(sM)^2}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$.
- [x] The **T-equivalent** uses $L_a = L_1 - M$, $L_b = M$, $L_c = L_2 - M$ (positive dot convention) — pure inductors, no coupling, amenable to standard nodal/mesh analysis.
- [x] At **DC steady state** ($s \to 0$): inductors act as short circuits, and $Z_{in} = R_1$ for any transformer — a quick sanity check.
- [x] At **high frequency** ($s \to \infty$): $Z_{in} \to sL_1 - \dfrac{s^2 M^2}{sL_2} = sL_1 - \dfrac{sM^2}{L_2} = s\left( L_1 - \dfrac{M^2}{L_2} \right)$ → frequency-dependent behaviour.
- [x] The **voltage transfer function** is $H(s) = \dfrac{sM \cdot Z_L}{Z_{11}(Z_{22}+Z_L) - (sM)^2}$.
- [x] Engineering uses: impedance matching (antennas), SMPS, audio isolation, EMI common-mode chokes, RF baluns.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Forgetting zero initial conditions when applying Laplace — KTU examiners frequently test this with a "switch closed at $t=0$" problem.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Misinterpreting $Z_{reflected}$ as *added* rather than *subtracted* — directly impacts the primary current calculation.
- [x] **Units check:** $s \cdot L$ must be in **ohms**, $s$ in **rad/s** (or $\text{s}^{-1}$), $L$ in **H**, $M$ in **H** — keep dimensions consistent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization of Two-Port Networks Using Z Parameters
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterization of Two-Port Networks Using Z Parameters

# Characterization of Two-Port Networks Using Z Parameters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Two-Port Network?

A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network enclosed within a closed boundary that contains *no independent sources*, accessed by **two pairs of terminals**. One pair is designated as the **input port** (Port 1) and the other as the **output port** (Port 2).

For a two-port network:
- **Port 1 (Input Port):** Has terminal current $I_1$ entering and terminal voltage $V_1$ across it.
- **Port 2 (Output Port):** Has terminal current $I_2$ entering and terminal voltage $V_2$ across it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A two-port network is a linear, bilateral, passive network represented by a 2x2 matrix relating its four terminal variables $(V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2)$ where only two variables are independent.

### 1.2 Definition of Z-Parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters)

**Z-parameters** (also known as **Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters**) are a set of network functions that relate the **port voltages** to the **port currents** when the ports are **left open-circuited** one at a time.

The defining equations are:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where the matrix $\begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ is called the **Z-parameter matrix** or **Open-Circuit Impedance Matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The units of all Z-parameters are **Ohms (Ω)** because they represent impedance. The subscripts follow the convention: the *first* subscript is the response port, and the *second* subscript is the driving port.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **black box** containing an unknown electronic circuit (like an amplifier, filter, or transmission line segment). You cannot see inside, but you want to characterize its behavior using only the four wires sticking out.

Think of the two-port network as a **four-terminal "appliance"** in your home:
- **Port 1** = The power input socket (where you plug in)
- **Port 2** = The output socket (where another device connects)
- **Z-parameters** are like the **electrical "fingerprint"** of that appliance — they tell you how the input voltage depends on the input current, how much the output voltage gets affected when you change input current, and so on.

The term *"open-circuit"* comes from the experimental setup: to measure these parameters, you **disconnect** (open-circuit) one port and apply a test signal at the other port. Just like you cannot measure a water tap's pressure without first closing other taps!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Meaning of Each Z-Parameter

Each Z-parameter has a precise physical interpretation obtained by setting one of the port currents to zero (open-circuiting the corresponding port):

### $Z_{11}$ — Driving-Point Impedance at Port 1
$$Z_{11} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$

This is the impedance measured at **Port 1** when **Port 2 is open-circuited**.

### $Z_{21}$ — Forward Transfer Impedance
$$Z_{21} = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$

This represents the **voltage produced at Port 2** per unit current at Port 1, with Port 2 open.

### $Z_{12}$ — Reverse Transfer Impedance
$$Z_{12} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$$

This represents the **voltage produced at Port 1** per unit current at Port 2, with Port 1 open.

### $Z_{22}$ — Driving-Point Impedance at Port 2
$$Z_{22} = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$$

This is the impedance measured at **Port 2** when **Port 1 is open-circuited**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Open-Circuit"?** The parameters are called *open-circuit* parameters because the test conditions require one of the ports to be open. This is in contrast to Y-parameters which require short-circuit conditions.

## 2.2 Conditions for Special Properties of Z-Parameters

### Reciprocity Condition
A two-port network is **reciprocal** if and only if:

$$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$

### Symmetry Condition
A two-port network is **symmetric** if it is reciprocal AND its input and output ports are interchangeable, which requires:

$$Z_{11} = Z_{22} \quad \text{(in addition to } Z_{12} = Z_{21} \text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every linear, passive, bilateral network is reciprocal**, so for such networks $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ always holds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Condition | Definition | Test Condition | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z_{11}$ | $V_1 / I_1 \vert_{I_2=0}$ | Port 2 open | Ω | Input driving-point impedance |
| $Z_{12}$ | $V_1 / I_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ | Port 1 open | Ω | Reverse transfer impedance |
| $Z_{21}$ | $V_2 / I_1 \vert_{I_2=0}$ | Port 2 open | Ω | Forward transfer impedance |
| $Z_{22}$ | $V_2 / I_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ | Port 1 open | Ω | Output driving-point impedance |
| Reciprocity | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | Always for linear passive bilateral networks | — | Network has symmetric behavior |
| Symmetry | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ and $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | Network is symmetric | — | Ports are interchangeable |
| Determinant | $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$ | Used in conversions | Ω² | Useful for parameter interconversion |
| Existence | $\Delta Z \neq 0$ | Network is non-degenerate | Ω² | Required for Z-parameters to exist |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Z-parameters are extensively used in:

1. **Transmission Line Analysis**: Characterizing sections of transmission lines for matching networks.
2. **Filter Design**: Analyzing LC ladder networks and lattice filters.
3. **Transistor Modeling**: Hybrid-pi and T-models of transistors at low frequencies.
4. **Power Systems**: Representing transformer equivalent circuits.
5. **Microwave Networks**: Cascade and series connection of two-port sub-networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Z-parameters are preferred over Y-parameters when the network contains series elements (like series-connected impedances), because **series interconnection of two-ports adds their Z-matrices directly**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Z-Parameter Equations from Network Equations

Let us start with a generalized two-port network. We have four terminal variables: $V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2$. Since the network is linear, by the **principle of superposition**, each voltage is a linear combination of the currents.

### Step 1: Apply Superposition

Consider $V_1$ as the dependent variable. It depends linearly on $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$V_1 = k_1 I_1 + k_2 I_2$$

Consider $V_2$ as the dependent variable. It also depends linearly on $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$V_2 = k_3 I_1 + k_4 I_2$$

### Step 2: Identify the Coefficients as Z-Parameters

When $I_2 = 0$ (Port 2 open-circuited), the equations reduce to:

$$V_1 \big|_{I_2=0} = k_1 I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_1 = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{I_2=0} = Z_{11}$$

$$V_2 \big|_{I_2=0} = k_3 I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_3 = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2=0} = Z_{21}$$

When $I_1 = 0$ (Port 1 open-circuited), the equations reduce to:

$$V_1 \big|_{I_1=0} = k_2 I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_2 = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1=0} = Z_{12}$$

$$V_2 \big|_{I_1=0} = k_4 I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_4 = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_2} \right|_{I_1=0} = Z_{22}$$

### Step 3: Write the Final Equations

$$\boxed{V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Z-Parameters for a T-Network

Consider the standard **T-Network** with series impedances $Z_A, Z_B$ in the two arms and shunt impedance $Z_C$ in the stem:

### Step 1: Apply KVL to the Two Loops

**Loop 1 (Primary Loop, with $I_1$ entering):**
$$V_1 = Z_A I_1 + Z_C (I_1 - I_2)$$

**Loop 2 (Secondary Loop, with $I_2$ entering):**
$$V_2 = Z_B I_2 + Z_C (I_2 - I_1)$$

### Step 2: Rearrange in Standard Z-Form

Expanding Loop 1 equation:

$$V_1 = (Z_A + Z_C) I_1 - Z_C I_2$$

Expanding Loop 2 equation:

$$V_2 = -Z_C I_1 + (Z_B + Z_C) I_2$$

### Step 3: Identify the Z-Parameters by Comparison

$$Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C \quad ; \quad Z_{12} = -Z_C$$

$$Z_{21} = -Z_C \quad ; \quad Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C$$

### Step 4: Verify Reciprocity

$$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = -Z_C \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms that the T-network is **reciprocal** (as expected for any passive linear network).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The negative sign in $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{21}$ arises because the reference direction of $I_2$ is conventionally taken as *entering* the port, while the actual current through the shunt arm $Z_C$ flows in the *opposite* direction relative to $I_1$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Z-Parameters for a Pi-Network

Consider the **Pi-Network** with shunt admittances $Y_A, Y_B$ at the two ports and series impedance $Z_C$ between them. Working with admittances is more convenient here, but since we need Z-parameters, we convert at the end.

### Step 1: Apply KCL at the Two Nodes

**Node 1 (Input Node):**
$$I_1 = Y_A V_1 + (V_1 - V_2) Y_{eq}$$

where $Y_{eq} = 1 / Z_C$.

**Node 2 (Output Node):**
$$I_2 = Y_B V_2 + (V_2 - V_1) Y_{eq}$$

### Step 2: Rearrange

$$I_1 = (Y_A + Y_{eq}) V_1 - Y_{eq} V_2$$

$$I_2 = -Y_{eq} V_1 + (Y_B + Y_{eq}) V_2$$

This gives the **Y-parameters**. To find Z-parameters, we must invert the Y-matrix.

### Step 3: Compute the Inverse

$$\Delta Y = (Y_A + Y_{eq})(Y_B + Y_{eq}) - Y_{eq}^2 = Y_A Y_B + Y_A Y_{eq} + Y_B Y_{eq}$$

### Step 4: Apply Matrix Inversion

$$Z_{11} = \frac{Y_B + Y_{eq}}{\Delta Y}$$

$$Z_{12} = \frac{Y_{eq}}{\Delta Y}$$

$$Z_{21} = \frac{Y_{eq}}{\Delta Y}$$

$$Z_{22} = \frac{Y_A + Y_{eq}}{\Delta Y}$$

Again, $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, confirming reciprocity.

## 3.4 Derivation of Series-Interconnection Z-Parameters

When two two-port networks N and M are connected in **series**, the resultant Z-matrix is the **sum** of their individual Z-matrices:

$$[Z]_{eq} = [Z]_N + [Z]_M$$

**Proof:**

For network N:
$$V_1^N = Z_{11}^N I_1 + Z_{12}^N I_2$$

For network M:
$$V_1^M = Z_{11}^M I_1 + Z_{12}^M I_2$$

When connected in series, the port voltages **add** while port currents remain the same:

$$V_1 = V_1^N + V_1^M = (Z_{11}^N + Z_{11}^M) I_1 + (Z_{12}^N + Z_{12}^M) I_2$$

Similar argument holds for $V_2$. Hence:

$$[Z] = [Z]^N + [Z]^M \quad \blacksquare$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a General Two-Port Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Port1["PORT 1 - INPUT"]
        V1node["V1"]
        I1node["I1 entering"]
    end
    subgraph TWOPORT["TWO PORT NETWORK BLACK BOX"]
        INTERN["Linear Passive Network containing Resistors Inductors Capacitors and dependent sources"]
    end
    subgraph Port2["PORT 2 - OUTPUT"]
        V2node["V2"]
        I2node["I2 entering"]
    end
    V1node -->|terminal 1 positive| INTERN
    INTERN -->|terminal 1 negative| I1node
    INTERN -->|terminal 2 positive| V2node
    V2node -->|terminal 2 negative| INTERN2["return path"]
    INTERN2 --> I2node
    style TWOPORT fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px
    style Port1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff9900
    style Port2 fill:#e6f9e6,stroke:#009933
```

## 4.2 Z-Parameter Characterization Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: Two Port Network Given"] --> Step1["Step 1: Identify the four variables V1 I1 V2 I2"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Open circuit Port 2 - set I2 = 0"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Apply test current I1 at Port 1"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Measure V1 and V2"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Z11 equals V1 over I1 and Z21 equals V2 over I1"]
    Step5 --> Step6["Step 3: Open circuit Port 1 - set I1 = 0"]
    Step6 --> Step7["Apply test current I2 at Port 2"]
    Step7 --> Step8["Measure V1 and V2"]
    Step8 --> Step9["Z12 equals V1 over I2 and Z22 equals V2 over I2"]
    Step9 --> Step10["Step 4: Form the 2x2 Z Matrix"]
    Step10 --> Step11["Step 5: Check Reciprocity Z12 equals Z21"]
    Step11 --> Step12["Step 6: Check Symmetry Z11 equals Z22"]
    Step12 --> End["Final: Characterized Network"]
    style Start fill:#ffe6e6
    style End fill:#e6ffe6
    style Step5 fill:#fffacd
    style Step9 fill:#fffacd
```

## 4.3 T-Network Architecture with Z-Parameter Identification

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Tnet["T NETWORK TOPOLOGY"]
        A["Terminal 1 plus - V1"] --> ZA["Series Arm ZA"]
        ZA --> NodeTop["Central Node"]
        NodeTop --> ZC["Shunt Stem ZC to Ground"]
        NodeTop --> ZB["Series Arm ZB"]
        ZB --> B["Terminal 2 plus - V2"]
        A --> G1["Terminal 1 minus - Ground"]
        B --> G2["Terminal 2 minus - Ground"]
    end
    subgraph Zmap["Z PARAMETER MAPPING"]
        Z11out["Z11 equals ZA plus ZC"]
        Z12out["Z12 equals minus ZC"]
        Z21out["Z21 equals minus ZC"]
        Z22out["Z22 equals ZB plus ZC"]
    end
    ZA -.-> Z11out
    ZC -.-> Z11out
    ZC -.-> Z12out
    ZC -.-> Z21out
    ZB -.-> Z22out
    ZC -.-> Z22out
    style Tnet fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px
    style Zmap fill:#fffaf0,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Series Interconnection of Two-Port Networks

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph NetA["NETWORK N"]
        A1["Port 1 top"] --- A2["Port 1 bot"]
        A3["Port 2 top"] --- A4["Port 2 bot"]
    end
    subgraph NetB["NETWORK M"]
        B1["Port 1 top"] --- B2["Port 1 bot"]
        B3["Port 2 top"] --- B4["Port 2 bot"]
    end
    A1 -.series connection.-> B1
    A2 -.series connection.-> B2
    A3 -.series connection.-> B3
    A4 -.series connection.-> B4
    Output["Resultant Z Matrix equals ZN plus ZM"]
    style NetA fill:#e0f7fa
    style NetB fill:#fff3e0
    style Output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:3px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Z-parameters of a two-port network. Why are they called open-circuit parameters?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):** Z-parameters (or open-circuit impedance parameters) are a set of four network parameters that relate the port voltages $V_1, V_2$ to the port currents $I_1, I_2$ of a two-port network through the equations:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

**Why called open-circuit (1 Mark):** They are called *open-circuit parameters* because each parameter is measured by **open-circuiting** one of the ports. For example, $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$ are found with Port 2 open ($I_2 = 0$), while $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22}$ are found with Port 1 open ($I_1 = 0$).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**State and explain the reciprocity condition for a two-port network in terms of Z-parameters.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Statement (1 Mark):** A two-port network is reciprocal if and only if $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$.

**Explanation (2 Marks):** This means that the forward transfer impedance (voltage at Port 2 per unit current at Port 1) equals the reverse transfer impedance (voltage at Port 1 per unit current at Port 2). For any **linear, passive, and bilateral** network (containing only R, L, C and ideal transformers), this condition is **automatically satisfied**. Violation of $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ indicates the presence of dependent sources or non-reciprocal elements like gyrators.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving Z-parameter problems, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to apply the correct **open-circuit condition** at the unused port.
> 2. Confuse the **sign convention** — both $I_1$ and $I_2$ must be taken as *entering* the network.
> 3. Mix up Z-parameters with Y-parameters (remember: Z-parameters → open-circuit, Y-parameters → short-circuit).
> 4. Lose marks by not explicitly writing the **final 2×2 matrix** in proper box format.
> 5. Forget to mention the **units (Ohms)** in the answer.

### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the Z-parameters of a T-network having series arms $Z_A$ and $Z_B$ and a shunt arm $Z_C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the T-network in part (a), find the condition under which the network is **symmetric**, and state the Z-matrix in that case. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Draw the circuit and label variables.**

The T-network has impedance $Z_A$ between terminal 1 and the central node, impedance $Z_B$ between the central node and terminal 2, and $Z_C$ between the central node and ground (common reference).

**Step 2: Apply KVL to the two loops.**

The current through $Z_C$ is $(I_1 - I_2)$ because $I_1$ enters the node and $I_2$ leaves.

Loop 1 (input loop):

$$V_1 = Z_A I_1 + Z_C (I_1 - I_2)$$

Loop 2 (output loop):

$$V_2 = Z_B I_2 + Z_C (I_2 - I_1)$$

**Step 3: Expand and rearrange in standard Z-form.**

$$V_1 = (Z_A + Z_C) I_1 + (-Z_C) I_2$$

$$V_2 = (-Z_C) I_1 + (Z_B + Z_C) I_2$$

**Step 4: Compare with the standard form** $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$, $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$:

$$\boxed{Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C, \quad Z_{12} = -Z_C, \quad Z_{21} = -Z_C, \quad Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C}$$

The Z-matrix is:

$$[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} Z_A + Z_C & -Z_C \\ -Z_C & Z_B + Z_C \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Units: Ohms)}$$

**Part (b) — Symmetry Condition (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Reciprocity Condition.**

For reciprocal network: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$

$$-Z_C = -Z_C \quad \checkmark \text{ (always satisfied)}$$

**Step 2: Symmetry requires additionally** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$.

$$Z_A + Z_C = Z_B + Z_C$$

$$\boxed{Z_A = Z_B}$$

**Step 3: Final Z-matrix under symmetry.**

When $Z_A = Z_B$:

$$[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} Z_A + Z_C & -Z_C \\ -Z_C & Z_A + Z_C \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Units: Ohms)}$$

**Conclusion:** A T-network is symmetric when its two series arms are equal ($Z_A = Z_B$). Under this condition, Port 1 and Port 2 become completely interchangeable, and the network exhibits the same driving-point impedance whether viewed from either port.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the four Z-parameters: $Z_{11}$, $Z_{12}$, $Z_{21}$, and $Z_{22}$, with proper test conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two two-port networks with Z-matrices $[Z]_A = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 2 \\ 2 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega$ and $[Z]_B = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega$ are connected in **series**. Determine the Z-matrix of the overall combination. Also verify whether the resultant network is reciprocal. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Definition | Test Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Z_{11}$ | $V_1/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ | Port 2 open | Ratio of input voltage to input current when output is open-circuited. Called the **input driving-point impedance**. |
| $Z_{21}$ | $V_2/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ | Port 2 open | Ratio of output voltage to input current when output is open-circuited. Called the **forward transfer impedance**; it represents forward voltage gain under open-circuit condition. |
| $Z_{12}$ | $V_1/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ | Port 1 open | Ratio of input voltage to output current when input is open-circuited. Called the **reverse transfer impedance**; represents reverse coupling. |
| $Z_{22}$ | $V_2/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ | Port 1 open | Ratio of output voltage to output current when input is open-circuited. Called the **output driving-point impedance**. |

**Step 2 (additional 1 mark for the explanation):** For any linear passive bilateral network, $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, meaning the forward and reverse transfer impedances are identical. This is the **principle of reciprocity**.

**Part (b) — Series Interconnection (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Apply the series-connection rule.**

For two two-port networks connected in series, the Z-matrices add algebraically:

$$[Z]_{overall} = [Z]_A + [Z]_B$$

**Step 2: Perform the matrix addition.**

$$[Z]_{overall} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 2 \\ 2 & 8 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$[Z]_{overall} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 + 5 & 2 + 1 \\ 2 + 1 & 8 + 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 3 \\ 3 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega$$

**Step 3: Verify reciprocity.**

For the overall network:

$$(Z_{12})_{overall} = 3 \, \Omega$$

$$(Z_{21})_{overall} = 3 \, \Omega$$

Since $(Z_{12})_{overall} = (Z_{21})_{overall} = 3 \, \Omega$, the **reciprocity condition is satisfied**.

$$\boxed{[Z]_{overall} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 3 \\ 3 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega \quad \text{and the network is reciprocal.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Series Connection:** Students often incorrectly assume series connection requires the Y-matrices or h-parameters to add. Remember: **Z-matrices add for series, Y-matrices add for parallel, and ABCD-matrices multiply for cascade**. This is a high-weightage KTU question — get this right!

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Z-Parameters**

- ✅ Z-parameters are also called **Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters** because they are measured by open-circuiting one port at a time.
- ✅ Units of all Z-parameters are **Ohms (Ω)** since they represent impedance.
- ✅ The defining equations are $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$.
- ✅ **$Z_{11}$** = Input driving-point impedance (Port 2 open); **$Z_{22}$** = Output driving-point impedance (Port 1 open).
- ✅ **$Z_{21}$** = Forward transfer impedance; **$Z_{12}$** = Reverse transfer impedance.
- ✅ **Reciprocity condition:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (always true for linear passive bilateral networks).
- ✅ **Symmetry condition:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ AND $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (network ports are interchangeable).
- ✅ **T-Network Z-parameters:** $Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = -Z_C$, $Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C$.
- ✅ **Series Connection Rule:** Z-matrices of individual two-ports add directly → $[Z]_{eq} = [Z]_1 + [Z]_2$.
- ✅ **Z-parameters exist** when the determinant $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} \neq 0$.
- ✅ Z-parameters are preferred for networks with **series-connected elements**.
- ✅ For KTU numericals, always apply **open-circuit** condition correctly and write the final answer in a boxed 2×2 matrix with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid and Transmission Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid and Transmission Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Port Network Parameters — Hybrid and Transmission Families**
>
> A **two-port network** is an electrical network with two distinct access points: an **input port** (port 1: $V_1, I_1$) and an **output port** (port 2: $V_2, I_2$). The network is characterized by four terminal variables, of which two are independent and two are dependent, leading to **six possible parameter sets** (z, y, h, g, T, T′). This module focuses on the **Hybrid (h, g)** and **Transmission (ABCD, A′B′C′D′)** families, which are especially suited for **transistor modeling** and **cascaded network analysis** respectively.

The four terminal variables are:

$$V_1, \, I_1, \, V_2, \, I_2$$

with the convention that **currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow into the network** at their respective ports.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Black Box Translator Booth"**
>
> Imagine a translation booth at an international conference. People on **side A (Port 1)** speak only English, and people on **side B (Port 2)** speak only French. The translator inside the booth takes what is "said" (voltages) and "responded" (currents) on one side and produces the equivalent signal on the other side.
>
> - **Hybrid parameters (h, g):** The translator works with a *mixed* mode — one side gives **voltage**, the other side gives **current**. This is exactly how a **transistor** behaves: the input is voltage-controlled, the output is current-controlled.
> - **Transmission parameters (ABCD):** The translator receives signals on side B and produces equivalent signals on side A — perfect for **cascading** multiple booths (networks) one after another, like a relay race.

## 1.3 The Six Two-Port Parameter Families (Overview)

| Family | Independent Variables | Dependent Variables | Best Suited For |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Z-parameters** | $I_1, I_2$ | $V_1, V_2$ | Series-connected networks |
| **Y-parameters** | $V_1, V_2$ | $I_1, I_2$ | Parallel-connected networks |
| **Hybrid (h)** | $I_1, V_2$ | $V_1, I_2$ | **Transistor (BJT/FET) models** |
| **Hybrid (g)** | $V_1, I_2$ | $I_1, V_2$ | Inverse hybrid (series-parallel) |
| **Transmission (T)** | $V_2, -I_2$ | $V_1, I_1$ | **Cascaded networks** |
| **Inverse Transmission (T′)** | $V_1, I_1$ | $V_2, -I_2$ | Reverse-cascaded networks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hybrid Parameters (h-Parameters) — The Transistor's Language

### 2.1.1 Defining Equations

The **h-parameters** (also called **hybrid** or **$h$-parameters** of the common-emitter configuration) are defined as:

$$V_1 = h_{11} \, I_1 + h_{12} \, V_2$$

$$I_2 = h_{21} \, I_1 + h_{22} \, V_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.1.2 Physical Meaning of Each Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each $h$-parameter has a unique physical meaning obtained by open-circuiting or short-circuiting one port:**

- $h_{11} = \left. \dfrac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$ = **Input impedance** with output **short-circuited** (units: ohms, $\Omega$).
- $h_{12} = \left. \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$ = **Reverse voltage gain** with input **open-circuited** (dimensionless).
- $h_{21} = \left. \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$ = **Forward current gain** with output **short-circuited** (dimensionless).
- $h_{22} = \left. \dfrac{I_2}{V_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$ = **Output admittance** with input **open-circuited** (units: siemens, $\text{S}$).

### 2.1.3 Equivalent Circuit

The **h-parameter equivalent circuit** consists of a **voltage-controlled voltage source** ($h_{12}V_2$) in series with an input resistance ($h_{11}$), and a **current-controlled current source** ($h_{21}I_1$) in parallel with an output admittance ($h_{22}$).

## 2.2 Inverse Hybrid Parameters (g-Parameters)

The **g-parameters** are the dual of $h$-parameters, used when the input is naturally a voltage port and the output is naturally a current port:

$$I_1 = g_{11} \, V_1 + g_{12} \, I_2$$

$$V_2 = g_{21} \, V_1 + g_{22} \, I_2$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [g] \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $g$-parameters:**
> - $g_{11} = \left. \dfrac{I_1}{V_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$ → Input admittance (output open).
> - $g_{12} = \left. \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} \right|_{V_1 = 0}$ → Reverse current gain (input shorted).
> - $g_{21} = \left. \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$ → Forward voltage gain (output open).
> - $g_{22} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_2} \bigg\vert_{V_1=0}$ → Output impedance (input shorted).

The relation between $h$ and $g$ matrices: $[g] = [h]^{-1}$, provided $\det[h] \neq 0$.

## 2.3 Transmission Parameters (ABCD Parameters) — The Cascader's Delight

### 2.3.1 Defining Equations

The **transmission (or ABCD) parameters** express the **input quantities** ($V_1, I_1$) in terms of the **output quantities** ($V_2, I_2$ where $-I_2$ flows **out** of the port):

$$V_1 = A \, V_2 - B \, I_2$$

$$I_1 = C \, V_2 - D \, I_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [T] \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the minus sign on $I_2$?**
> The minus sign convention arises because in **cascaded** networks, the current flowing out of one sub-network equals the current flowing into the next, but the standard reference for $I_2$ in each sub-network is into the port. The negative sign makes the chain multiplication work consistently: $[T]_{total} = [T]_1 \cdot [T]_2 \cdot [T]_3$.

### 2.3.2 Physical Meaning

- $A = \left. \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$ → **Reverse voltage gain** (open-circuit), dimensionless.
- $B = -\left. \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$ → **Negative short-circuit transfer impedance** (units: $\Omega$).
- $C = \left. \dfrac{I_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$ → **Reverse transfer admittance** (units: $\text{S}$).
- $D = -\left. \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$ → **Negative short-circuit current gain**, dimensionless.

### 2.3.3 Determinantal Property

For any **reciprocal, linear, lumped, passive** network, the transmission matrix satisfies:

$$AD - BC = 1$$

This is the **reciprocity condition** for $T$-parameters.

## 2.4 Inverse Transmission Parameters (A′B′C′D′)

The **inverse transmission** parameters express output in terms of input — useful for **reverse-cascaded** chains:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = [T'] \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Relation: $[T'] = [T]^{-1}$ for reciprocal networks, meaning $A' = D, \; B' = B, \; C' = C, \; D' = A$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table of Conversion Formulas (2-Port Parameter Inter-Relations)**

> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| From / To | **Z-params** | **Y-params** | **h-params** | **g-params** | **T-params (ABCD)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Z-params** $Z_{11}, Z_{12}, Z_{21}, Z_{22}$ | — | $Y = Z^{-1}$ | $h_{11} = \frac{\det Z}{Z_{22}}$, $h_{12} = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}}$ | $g_{11} = \frac{1}{Z_{11}}$, $g_{12} = -\frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{11}}$ | $A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}$, $B = \frac{\det Z}{Z_{21}}$ |
| **Y-params** | $Z = Y^{-1}$ | — | $h_{11} = \frac{1}{Y_{11}}$, $h_{12} = -\frac{Y_{12}}{Y_{11}}$ | $g_{11} = \frac{\det Y}{Y_{22}}$, $g_{12} = \frac{Y_{12}}{Y_{22}}$ | $A = -\frac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}}$, $B = -\frac{1}{Y_{21}}$ |
| **h-params** | $Z_{11} = \frac{\det h}{h_{22}}$, $Z_{12} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}$ | $Y_{11} = \frac{1}{h_{11}}$, $Y_{12} = -\frac{h_{12}}{h_{11}}$ | — | $g = h^{-1}$ | $A = -\frac{\det h}{h_{21}}$, $B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}$ |
| **T-params** | $Z_{11} = \frac{A}{C}$, $Z_{12} = \frac{\det T}{C}$ | $Y_{11} = \frac{D}{B}$, $Y_{12} = -\frac{\det T}{B}$ | $h_{11} = \frac{B}{D}$, $h_{12} = \frac{\det T}{D}$ | $g_{11} = \frac{C}{A}$, $g_{12} = -\frac{\det T}{A}$ | — |

> **where $\det h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$ and $\det T = AD - BC$**.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **h-parameters**: Indispensable in **small-signal BJT/JFET analysis**, RF amplifier design, and microwave transistor datasheets (S-parameters are h-parameters extended to high frequency).
- **ABCD parameters**: Used in **transmission line theory**, **filter design cascades**, **microwave networks** (where they become $S$-parameters), and **power systems** for analyzing long transmission lines using the equivalent-$\pi$ model.
- **g-parameters**: Used in **FET modeling** where the input is a high-impedance voltage port.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from h-Parameters to T-Parameters

We begin with the $h$-parameter equations:

$$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1: Solve (ii) for $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$:**

$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \implies I_1 = \frac{I_2 - h_{22} V_2}{h_{21}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute this $I_1$ into (i) to find $V_1$:**

$$V_1 = h_{11} \left( \frac{I_2 - h_{22} V_2}{h_{21}} \right) + h_{12} V_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2 - \frac{h_{11} h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 + h_{12} V_2$$

**Step 3: Group the $V_2$ terms together:**

$$V_1 = -\frac{\det h}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2$$

where $\det h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$.

**Step 4: Compare with the standard T-parameter form** $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$:

$$A = -\frac{\det h}{h_{21}}, \qquad B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}$$

> *(Note: Since the standard form is $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ and we have $V_1 = -\frac{\det h}{h_{21}}V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}I_2$, we identify $-B = \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}$ giving $B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}$.)*

**Step 5: Re-express the $I_1$ equation in the standard form** $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$:

$$I_1 = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{1}{h_{21}} I_2$$

**Step 6: Match coefficients:**

$$C = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}, \qquad D = -\frac{1}{h_{21}}$$

> **Note the sign convention:** Some textbooks define $I_2$ flowing **out** of the network, in which case the $T$-matrix becomes $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\det h/h_{21} & -h_{11}/h_{21} \\ -h_{22}/h_{21} & -1/h_{21} \end{bmatrix}$. KTU typically follows the $I_2$-into-the-port convention shown above.

## 3.2 Derivation: h-Parameters of a Standard T-Network

Consider a **T-network** with series arms $Z_a$ (left), $Z_b$ (right), and shunt arm $Z_c$ (middle), terminated in a load $Z_L$.

**Mesh Equations** (writing KVL around the input and output meshes, with output current flowing into the network):

**Mesh 1 (Input):**
$$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) I_1 + Z_c I_2$$

**Mesh 2 (Output):**
$$V_2 = Z_c I_1 + (Z_b + Z_c) I_2$$

**Step 1: Match the equations with the h-parameter form** $V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$:

From Mesh 1, we have $V_1$ in terms of $I_1, I_2$. We need $V_1$ in terms of $I_1, V_2$. From Mesh 2:

$$I_2 = \frac{V_2 - Z_c I_1}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

Substitute into Mesh 1:

$$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) I_1 + Z_c \cdot \frac{V_2 - Z_c I_1}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

**Step 2: Group the $I_1$ and $V_2$ terms:**

$$V_1 = \left[ (Z_a + Z_c) - \frac{Z_c^2}{Z_b + Z_c} \right] I_1 + \frac{Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c} V_2$$

**Step 3: Simplify the $I_1$ coefficient:**

$$(Z_a + Z_c) - \frac{Z_c^2}{Z_b + Z_c} = \frac{(Z_a + Z_c)(Z_b + Z_c) - Z_c^2}{Z_b + Z_c} = \frac{Z_a Z_b + Z_a Z_c + Z_b Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c} = \frac{Z_a Z_b + Z_c(Z_a + Z_b)}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

**Step 4: Identify $h_{11}$ and $h_{12}$:**

$$h_{11} = \frac{Z_a Z_b + Z_c(Z_a + Z_b)}{Z_b + Z_c} = \frac{Z_a(Z_b + Z_c) + Z_b Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c} = Z_a + \frac{Z_b Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

$$h_{12} = \frac{Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

**Step 5: Find $h_{21}$ and $h_{22}$ from the output equation** $I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$:

Re-writing Mesh 2 solved for $I_2$:

$$I_2 = -\frac{Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c} I_1 + \frac{1}{Z_b + Z_c} V_2$$

Therefore:

$$h_{21} = -\frac{Z_c}{Z_b + Z_c}, \qquad h_{22} = \frac{1}{Z_b + Z_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For a purely resistive T-network with $Z_a = Z_b = R_1$ and $Z_c = R_2$:
> - $h_{11} = R_1 + \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{R_1^2 + 2R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
> - $h_{22} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 + R_2}$
> - $h_{12} = h_{21} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (magnitude symmetry confirms reciprocity).

## 3.3 Derivation: ABCD Parameters of a Symmetric T-Network

A **symmetric T-network** has $Z_a = Z_b$ (equal series arms). Using the same mesh equations from §3.2:

$$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) I_1 + Z_c I_2$$
$$V_2 = Z_c I_1 + (Z_a + Z_c) I_2$$

**Step 1: Solve for $V_1$ and $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$:**

From the second equation, $I_1 = \dfrac{V_2 - (Z_a + Z_c) I_2}{Z_c}$.

**Step 2: Substitute into the first equation:**

$$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) \cdot \frac{V_2 - (Z_a + Z_c) I_2}{Z_c} + Z_c I_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} V_2 - \frac{(Z_a + Z_c)^2}{Z_c} I_2 + Z_c I_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} V_2 - \left[ \frac{(Z_a + Z_c)^2 - Z_c^2}{Z_c} \right] I_2$$

**Step 3: Simplify the $I_2$ coefficient:**

$$(Z_a + Z_c)^2 - Z_c^2 = Z_a^2 + 2 Z_a Z_c = Z_a(Z_a + 2 Z_c)$$

So: $V_1 = \dfrac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} V_2 - \dfrac{Z_a(Z_a + 2 Z_c)}{Z_c} I_2$.

**Step 4: Match with $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$:**

$$A = \frac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} = 1 + \frac{Z_a}{Z_c}$$

$$B = \frac{Z_a(Z_a + 2 Z_c)}{Z_c} = Z_a + \frac{2 Z_a Z_c}{Z_c} = Z_a + \frac{Z_a^2 + 2 Z_a Z_c}{Z_c}$$

> *(For a clean result: $B = \dfrac{Z_a^2 + 2 Z_a Z_c}{Z_c} = \dfrac{Z_a^2}{Z_c} + 2 Z_a$)*

**Step 5: Express $I_1$:**

$$I_1 = \frac{V_2}{Z_c} - \frac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} I_2$$

So: $C = \dfrac{1}{Z_c}, \quad D = \dfrac{Z_a + Z_c}{Z_c} = 1 + \dfrac{Z_a}{Z_c}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Check:** $AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{Z_a}{Z_c}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{Z_a^2}{Z_c} + 2Z_a\right)\cdot \frac{1}{Z_c}$
> 
> $AD - BC = 1 + \frac{2 Z_a}{Z_c} + \frac{Z_a^2}{Z_c^2} - \frac{Z_a^2}{Z_c^2} - \frac{2 Z_a}{Z_c} = 1$ ✓

## 3.4 Derivation: h-Parameters of a CE Transistor (Standard Textbook Values)

For a typical **small-signal BJT in common-emitter** configuration, the $h$-parameter values are of the order of:

$$h_{11} \approx 1.1 \, \text{k}\Omega, \quad h_{12} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4}, \quad h_{21} \approx 50, \quad h_{22} \approx 25 \, \mu\text{S}$$

**Step 1: Calculate $\det h$:**

$$\det h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = (1.1 \times 10^3)(25 \times 10^{-6}) - (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)$$

$$\det h = 0.0275 - 0.0125 = 0.015$$

**Step 2: Convert to T-parameters using the formulas from §3.1:**

$$A = -\frac{\det h}{h_{21}} = -\frac{0.015}{50} = -3 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} = -\frac{1100}{50} = -22 \, \Omega$$

$$C = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} = -\frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{50} = -0.5 \, \mu\text{S}$$

$$D = -\frac{1}{h_{21}} = -\frac{1}{50} = -0.02$$

**Step 3: Verify reciprocity:** $AD - BC = (-3 \times 10^{-4})(-0.02) - (-22)(-0.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 6 \times 10^{-6} - 1.1 \times 10^{-5} = -5 \times 10^{-6}$

> The non-unity result is expected because a **transistor is non-reciprocal** (it amplifies in one direction only) — $AD - BC \neq 1$ is a direct indicator of non-reciprocity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeINP[Input Port 1: V1, I1] --> nodeH11[Resistance h11 in series]
    nodeH11 --> nodeSumV[V1 measurement node]
    nodeVCVS[VCVS: h12 times V2] --> nodeSumV
    nodeSumV --> nodeGND1[Reference ground at port 1]
    
    nodeSumV --> nodePort1Out[Continues to internal network]
    
    nodePort2In[Output Port 2: V2, I2] --> nodeH22[Admittance h22 shunt to ground]
    nodePort2In --> nodeGND2[Reference ground at port 2]
    
    nodeCCCS[CCCS: h21 times I1] --> nodePort2In
    
    style nodeINP fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeVCVS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeCCCS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeH11 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeH22 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Diagram:** This block-level schematic shows the canonical two-port h-parameter equivalent model. The input port contains a series resistance $h_{11}$ and a voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS) of value $h_{12}V_2$. The output port contains a current-controlled current source (CCCS) of value $h_{21}I_1$ in parallel with the output admittance $h_{22}$. This is the **standard hybrid-pi model** used in BJT small-signal analysis.

## 4.2 ABCD Parameter Flow for Cascaded Two-Port Networks

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSRC[Source Vs, Zs] --> nodeP1A[Port 1 of Network A]
    nodeP1A --> nodeMAT1[Matrix TA equals A B C D]
    nodeMAT1 --> nodeP2A[Port 2 of Network A]
    nodeP2A --> nodeP1B[Port 1 of Network B]
    nodeP1B --> nodeMAT2[Matrix TB equals A B C D]
    nodeMAT2 --> nodeP2B[Port 2 of Network B]
    nodeP2B --> nodeLOAD[Load ZL]
    
    nodeMAT3[Total Matrix Ttotal equals TA times TB] -.-> nodeP1A
    
    style nodeSRC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLOAD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeMAT1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeMAT2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeMAT3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Diagram:** This architecture illustrates the **cascading principle** of ABCD parameters. Each sub-network A and B is represented by its respective transmission matrix $T_A$ and $T_B$. The total transmission matrix is simply the matrix product: $T_{total} = T_A \cdot T_B$. This is the fundamental reason why ABCD parameters are preferred in cascaded analysis — no matrix inversion is required.

## 4.3 Two-Port Network Port Variable Reference Convention

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP1[PORT 1: Input Side] --> nodeV1[V1: voltage across port 1]
    nodeP1 --> nodeI1[I1: current entering port 1]
    nodeP2[PORT 2: Output Side] --> nodeV2[V2: voltage across port 2]
    nodeP2 --> nodeI2[I2: current entering port 2, but flows out as minus I2 for T params]
    
    nodeZ[Z params: V equals Z times I] -.uses.-> nodeV1
    nodeZ -.uses.-> nodeV2
    nodeY[Y params: I equals Y times V] -.uses.-> nodeI1
    nodeY -.uses.-> nodeI2
    nodeH[h params: mixed V1 I2 depends on I1 V2] -.uses.-> nodeV1
    nodeH -.uses.-> nodeI2
    nodeT[T params: input depends on output, with minus I2 sign] -.uses.-> nodeV2
    nodeT -.uses.-> nodeI2
    
    style nodeZ fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeY fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Diagram:** The schematic displays the **port variable reference conventions** used universally in two-port network theory. The choice of independent vs. dependent variables directly dictates which parameter family is being used. Notice the unique role of $-I_2$ in T-parameters — this negative sign is what enables the matrix chain multiplication property.

## 4.4 Conversion Flowchart: When to Use Which Parameter Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSTART[Given Two-Port Network] --> nodeQ1{What is the natural independent variable at input?}
    nodeQ1 -->|Current I1| nodeQ1A{Output port type?}
    nodeQ1A -->|Current I2| nodeZPATH[Use Z parameters: V equals Z I]
    nodeQ1A -->|Voltage V2| nodeHPATH[Use h parameters: V1 and I2 are dependent]
    
    nodeQ1 -->|Voltage V1| nodeQ1B{Output port type?}
    nodeQ1B -->|Current I2| nodeGPATH[Use g parameters: I1 and V2 are dependent]
    nodeQ1B -->|Voltage V2| nodeYPATH[Use Y parameters: I equals Y V]
    
    nodeQ1 -->|Network is cascaded| nodeTPATH[Use T parameters for chain multiplication]
    
    style nodeZPATH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeHPATH fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeGPATH fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeYPATH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeTPATH fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Diagram:** A **decision flowchart** that helps a student select the appropriate parameter family based on the network's physical structure. The h-parameter path is highlighted in gold as the most relevant choice for transistor circuits, and the T-parameter path is highlighted in purple for cascaded filter and transmission-line networks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
