topics.title: Series and Parallel Connections of Two-Port Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Series and Parallel Connections of Two-Port Networks # Series and Parallel Connections of Two-Port Networks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Two-Port Network** is a linear, bilateral electrical network containing two accessible pairs of terminals (ports), enclosed within a "black box" and accessible to the external world only through its ports. The **input port (Port 1)** has terminal variables $V_1$ and $I_1$, while the **output port (Port 2)** has terminal variables $V_2$ and $I_2$. **Series Connection of Two-Port Networks** is a specific cascade-free interconnection where the output ports of two (or more) two-port networks are connected in series such that the **same current flows through both output ports** and the **same current flows through both input ports**, effectively forcing the port currents to be additive. **Parallel Connection of Two-Port Networks** is the dual interconnection where input ports are connected in parallel and output ports are connected in parallel, forcing the **same voltage across input ports** and the **same voltage across output ports**, while the port currents become additive. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The fundamental insight is that the *type of parameter* used to characterize each sub-network determines the *type of combination*: > - **Series** → use **Z-parameters** (impedance representation) > - **Parallel** → use **Y-parameters** (admittance representation) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine two water pipelines joined end-to-end (series). The *water flow rate* (analogous to current) is the same throughout, but the *pressure drops* (analogous to voltage) add up. This is exactly why **Z-parameters (which relate voltages to currents) are additive** in series — the total voltage drop equals the sum of individual drops. Conversely, imagine two water pipes carrying flow side-by-side (parallel) from the same reservoir to the same outlet. The *pressure* (voltage) is identical across both, but the *total flow rate* (current) splits and recombines. This is why **Y-parameters (which relate currents to voltages) are additive** in parallel. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in real engineering:** When you cascade amplifiers, design multi-stage filters, or combine transmission line sections, you are physically wiring two-port networks in series or parallel. Engineers must know whether to *add Z-matrices* or *add Y-matrices* to predict the combined behavior. ### 1.3 Reference Conventions All four standard two-port parameters (Z, Y, h, g, T) are defined under the **standard reference convention** where: - $I_1$ enters Port 1 (+) - $I_2$ enters Port 2 (+) - $V_1$ is measured across Port 1 (+ on top terminal) - $V_2$ is measured across Port 2 (+ on top terminal) > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfall:** Using non-standard current direction (e.g., $I_2$ leaving Port 2) will flip the signs of every term in your parameter matrix. Always draw the network and label currents **entering** each port before applying any formula. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Recap: The Five Standard Two-Port Parameters A two-port network is fully characterized by **two of the four variables** $(V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2)$. The six possible combinations yield the standard parameter sets. Only three are independent because reciprocity and symmetry impose constraints. | Parameter Set | Defining Equations | Matrix Form | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Z-parameters (Open-circuit)** | $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$
$V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Series interconnections | | **Y-parameters (Short-circuit)** | $I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Parallel interconnections | | **h-parameters (Hybrid)** | $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Transistor models | | **g-parameters (Inverse Hybrid)** | $I_1 = g_{11}V_1 + g_{12}I_2$
$V_2 = g_{21}V_1 + g_{22}I_2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Cascade (with T) | | **T-parameters (ABCD)** | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$
$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Transmission lines, cascades | ## 2.2 Series Connection — The Logic **Goal:** Find a single equivalent two-port that represents two sub-networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ connected in series. **Logic Steps:** 1. The input ports of $N_a$ and $N_b$ are connected in series → a single loop exists at the input. By KVL, $V_1 = V_{1a} + V_{1b}$, and the same current $I_1$ flows through both. 2. The output ports of $N_a$ and $N_b$ are connected in series → a single loop exists at the output. By KVL, $V_2 = V_{2a} + V_{2b}$, and the same current $I_2$ flows through both. 3. Express each sub-network using Z-parameters: - $V_{1a} = Z_{11a}I_1 + Z_{12a}I_2$ and $V_{2a} = Z_{21a}I_1 + Z_{22a}I_2$ - $V_{1b} = Z_{11b}I_1 + Z_{12b}I_2$ and $V_{2b} = Z_{21b}I_1 + Z_{22b}I_2$ 4. Add the equations: - $V_1 = (Z_{11a} + Z_{11b})I_1 + (Z_{12a} + Z_{12b})I_2$ - $V_2 = (Z_{21a} + Z_{21b})I_1 + (Z_{22a} + Z_{22b})I_2$ 5. Identify the equivalent Z-matrix: $$ \boxed{Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b} $$ where $Z_{eq,ij} = Z_{a,ij} + Z_{b,ij}$ for $i,j \in \{1,2\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Bruton Test" of Validity:** A series connection is *only* valid if the resulting network still has **two accessible, distinct ports** — that is, no internal current paths are formed that bypass the intended port terminals. If the connection causes a portion of $I_1$ to flow where $I_2$ should flow, the test fails and matrix addition is invalid. ## 2.3 Parallel Connection — The Logic **Goal:** Find a single equivalent two-port for two sub-networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ connected in parallel. **Logic Steps:** 1. The input ports of $N_a$ and $N_b$ are connected in parallel → same voltage $V_1$ across both; currents add: $I_1 = I_{1a} + I_{1b}$. 2. The output ports are connected in parallel → same voltage $V_2$ across both; currents add: $I_2 = I_{2a} + I_{2b}$. 3. Express each sub-network using Y-parameters: - $I_{1a} = Y_{11a}V_1 + Y_{12a}V_2$ and $I_{2a} = Y_{21a}V_1 + Y_{22a}V_2$ - $I_{1b} = Y_{11b}V_1 + Y_{12b}V_2$ and $I_{2b} = Y_{21b}V_1 + Y_{22b}V_2$ 4. Add the equations: - $I_1 = (Y_{11a} + Y_{11b})V_1 + (Y_{12a} + Y_{12b})V_2$ - $I_2 = (Y_{21a} + Y_{21b})V_1 + (Y_{22a} + Y_{22b})V_2$ 5. Identify the equivalent Y-matrix: $$ \boxed{Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Validity Test (Bruton):** Same as series, but tested for parallel. A parallel connection is *only* valid if the two ports remain distinct and no internal loops form that violate KVL at the connecting nodes. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Series Connection | Parallel Connection | |---|---|---| | **Active parameter set** | Z-parameters | Y-parameters | | **Combining equation** | $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$ | $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$ | | **Port voltage relation** | $V_1 = V_{1a} + V_{1b}$, $V_2 = V_{2a} + V_{2b}$ | $V_1$ common, $V_2$ common | | **Port current relation** | $I_1$ common, $I_2$ common | $I_1 = I_{1a} + I_{1b}$, $I_2 = I_{2a} + I_{2b}$ | | **Validity condition** | Ports remain accessible (Bruton test) | Ports remain accessible (Bruton test) | | **Matrix form** | $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$ (direct sum) | $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$ (direct sum) | | **Conversion needed if other param given** | Convert to Z first: $Z = Y^{-1}$ | Convert to Y first: $Y = Z^{-1}$ | | **Reciprocity preserved?** | Yes, if $N_a$, $N_b$ are reciprocal | Yes, if $N_a$, $N_b$ are reciprocal | | **Symmetry preserved?** | Yes, if $N_a$, $N_b$ are symmetric | Yes, if $N_a$, $N_b$ are symmetric | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Multi-stage amplifier analysis:** Two amplifier stages often share a common ground and are connected in series from a signal-flow perspective. Their Z-parameters add to give the overall input impedance. - **Power systems:** Transmission lines and transformers modeled as two-ports are connected in series to form long-distance power links. - **Filter design:** Ladder networks (LC ladders) are constructed by series-connecting L-sections and π-sections, where the Z-parameters compose the overall response. - **Parallel operation of sources:** Two generators feeding a common bus bar form a parallel two-port; Y-parameters add to predict circulating currents and load sharing. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Series Connection via Z-Parameters **Setup:** Two two-port networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ are connected in series at both ports. Let the standard reference directions be $I_1$ entering Port 1 and $I_2$ entering Port 2 for **both** networks. **Step 1 — Write Z-parameter equations for $N_a$:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1a} &= Z_{11a} \, I_1 + Z_{12a} \, I_2 \\ V_{2a} &= Z_{21a} \, I_1 + Z_{22a} \, I_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write Z-parameter equations for $N_b$:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1b} &= Z_{11b} \, I_1 + Z_{12b} \, I_2 \\ V_{2b} &= Z_{21b} \, I_1 + Z_{22b} \, I_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply KVL at the input and output loops.** Because the input ports are in series, the same current $I_1$ flows through both, and the total voltage across the combined port is the sum: $$ V_1 = V_{1a} + V_{1b} $$ Similarly at the output: $$ V_2 = V_{2a} + V_{2b} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and combine.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= (Z_{11a} + Z_{11b})I_1 + (Z_{12a} + Z_{12b})I_2 \\ V_2 &= (Z_{21a} + Z_{21b})I_1 + (Z_{22a} + Z_{22b})I_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the equivalent Z-matrix.** By direct comparison with $V_1 = Z_{11,eq}I_1 + Z_{12,eq}I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21,eq}I_1 + Z_{22,eq}I_2$: $$ \boxed{Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b \quad \text{where} \quad Z_{ij,eq} = Z_{ij,a} + Z_{ij,b}} $$ This is a **direct matrix sum**, valid whenever the port currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the same in both sub-networks (which is the definition of series connection). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel Connection via Y-Parameters **Setup:** Two two-port networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ are connected in parallel at both ports. Both share the same $V_1$ and $V_2$. **Step 1 — Write Y-parameter equations for $N_a$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{1a} &= Y_{11a} \, V_1 + Y_{12a} \, V_2 \\ I_{2a} &= Y_{21a} \, V_1 + Y_{22a} \, V_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write Y-parameter equations for $N_b$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{1b} &= Y_{11b} \, V_1 + Y_{12b} \, V_2 \\ I_{2b} &= Y_{21b} \, V_1 + Y_{22b} \, V_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply KCL at the input and output nodes.** For parallel input ports, the currents add: $$ I_1 = I_{1a} + I_{1b} $$ For parallel output ports: $$ I_2 = I_{2a} + I_{2b} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and combine.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= (Y_{11a} + Y_{11b})V_1 + (Y_{12a} + Y_{12b})V_2 \\ I_2 &= (Y_{21a} + Y_{21b})V_1 + (Y_{22a} + Y_{22b})V_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the equivalent Y-matrix.** $$ \boxed{Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b \quad \text{where} \quad Y_{ij,eq} = Y_{ij,a} + Y_{ij,b}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** > Two identical T-networks are connected in series. Each T-network consists of $Z_1 = 10\ \Omega$ in each series arm and $Z_2 = 20\ \Omega$ as the shunt arm. Find the **Z-parameters of the equivalent two-port network**. **Step 1 — Find Z-parameters of a single T-network.** A T-network has the structure: $Z_1$ in series at the input, $Z_2$ as shunt (between the two series arms), and $Z_1$ in series at the output. Open-circuit input impedance at Port 1 (Port 2 open → no current through output $Z_1$): $$ Z_{11a} = Z_1 + Z_2 = 10 + 20 = 30\ \Omega $$ By symmetry, $Z_{22a} = Z_{11a} = 30\ \Omega$. Transfer impedance with Port 2 open: $$ Z_{21a} = Z_{12a} = Z_2 = 20\ \Omega $$ So: $$ Z_a = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 30 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ **Step 2 — Since $N_b$ is identical to $N_a$:** $$ Z_b = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 30 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ **Step 3 — Apply series combination formula $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$:** $$ Z_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 30+30 & 20+20 \\ 20+20 & 30+30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 60 & 40 \\ 40 & 60 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check (Bruton test):** The two T-networks stacked in series still have two accessible ports, with no internal current path bypassing the port terminals. The combination is valid. > **Result:** The equivalent two-port has $Z_{11} = Z_{22} = 60\ \Omega$ and $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 40\ \Omega$. This is also a T-network with series arms $Z_1' = 20\ \Omega$ and shunt $Z_2' = 40\ \Omega$, confirming the geometric expectation: stacking two identical T-networks in series doubles every arm. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conversion Between Parameter Sets When given a network characterized in one parameter set, the conversion formulas are essential. | From → To | Conversion Formula | |---|---| | Z → Y | $Y = Z^{-1}$ (matrix inverse) | | Y → Z | $Z = Y^{-1}$ (matrix inverse) | | Z → T (ABCD) | $A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}$, $B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}$, $C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}}$, $D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$ | | T → Z | $Z_{11} = \frac{A}{C}$, $Z_{12} = \frac{\Delta T}{C}$, $Z_{21} = \frac{1}{C}$, $Z_{22} = \frac{D}{C}$ | where $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$ and $\Delta T = AD - BC$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** If a problem gives you a hybrid (h) parameter network and asks for series connection, you **must first convert h → Z** using $Z = $ matrix expression, then add Z-matrices. Direct addition of h-matrices is meaningless. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series Connection of Two-Port Networks ```mermaid graph LR node1["PORT 1 Input Terminal A"] --> node2["Network Na Z-parameters"] node2 --> node3["Internal Series Junction 1"] node3 --> node4["Network Nb Z-parameters"] node4 --> node5["PORT 2 Output Terminal C"] node6["PORT 1 Input Terminal B"] --> node7["Internal Series Junction 2"] node7 --> node8["Internal Series Junction 3"] node8 --> node9["PORT 2 Output Terminal D"] node3 --- node7 style node2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node9 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** - The **top rail** (nodes 1→2→3→4→5) carries the upper conductor; voltage $V_1$ appears across the input (between nodes 1 and 6), and $V_2$ appears across the output (between nodes 5 and 9). - The **bottom rail** (nodes 6→7→8→9) carries the return conductor. - The connection `node3 --- node7` represents the **internal series junction** where the two sub-networks are joined in series. - The same current $I_1$ flows through both sub-networks at the input, and the same $I_2$ flows through both at the output. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Parallel Connection of Two-Port Networks ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Port 1 Top Terminal"] --> nodeB["Junction P1T"] nodeB --> nodeC["Network Na Y-parameters"] nodeB --> nodeD["Network Nb Y-parameters"] nodeC --> nodeE["Junction P2T"] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF["Port 2 Top Terminal"] nodeG["Port 1 Bottom Terminal"] --> nodeH["Junction P1B"] nodeH --> nodeI["Network Na Bottom Rail"] nodeH --> nodeJ["Network Nb Bottom Rail"] nodeI --> nodeK["Junction P2B"] nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL["Port 2 Bottom Terminal"] style nodeC fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeH fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeK fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** - **Yellow nodes** (B, E, H, K) are the **common parallel junction nodes** where the sub-networks are tied together. - **Blue nodes** (C, D, I, J) are the sub-networks themselves, connected between the same two nodes at the top and bottom. - The voltage $V_1$ is common between nodeB and nodeH; the voltage $V_2$ is common between nodeE and nodeK. - The currents $I_{1a}$ and $I_{1b}$ add at nodeB; similarly at the output. ## 4.3 Validity Test Flowchart (Bruton Test) ```mermaid graph TD step1["Start: Two two-port networks to be connected"] --> step2{"Identify connection type: Series or Parallel?"} step2 -->|"Series"| step3["Add Z-parameters: Zeq = Za + Zb"] step2 -->|"Parallel"| step4["Add Y-parameters: Yeq = Ya + Yb"] step3 --> step5["Apply Bruton Validity Test"] step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6{"Do the two ports remain distinct and accessible?"} step6 -->|"Yes"| step7["VALID - Use Zeq or Yeq as final answer"] step6 -->|"No - internal loops formed"| step8["INVALID - Connection not realizable as two-port"] step8 --> step9["Redesign connection or use T-parameters for cascade"] style step7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style step8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style step3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style step4 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Table: Series vs. Parallel — Visual Summary | Property | Series Connection | Parallel Connection | |---|---|---| | Active parameter | **Z-parameters** | **Y-parameters** | | Combining rule | $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$ | $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$ | | Physical intuition | Same current, voltages add | Same voltage, currents add | | Governing law | KVL (voltage sum) | KCL (current sum) | | Most common application | Cascaded line sections, multi-stage filters | Multi-source paralleling, parallel filters | | Conversion prerequisite | None (already Z) | None (already Y) | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Q1. Why are Z-parameters used for series connection of two-port networks and Y-parameters used for parallel connection?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In a series connection, the same current $I_1$ flows through both input ports and the same current $I_2$ flows through both output ports (KVL applies — voltages add). Since Z-parameters express voltages as linear functions of currents ($V = ZI$), the voltage contributions from each sub-network add directly, leading to $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$. In a parallel connection, the same voltage $V_1$ appears across both input ports and $V_2$ across both output ports (KCL applies — currents add). Since Y-parameters express currents as linear functions of voltages ($I = YV$), the current contributions from each sub-network add directly, giving $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Q2. State the Bruton test for the validity of series and parallel connections of two-port networks.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Bruton validity test** states that a series or parallel connection of two-port networks is *valid* only if, after interconnection, the resulting network still has **two distinct, accessible ports**. The connection becomes invalid if internal loops or current paths form that cause the port currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ to no longer be the same in all sub-networks (for series) or if the port voltages are no longer identical (for parallel). In such invalid cases, the simple matrix addition $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$ or $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$ does **not** hold. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 4]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the relationship for finding the Z-parameters of a two-port network formed by the **series connection of two two-port networks** $N_a$ and $N_b$. Mention the condition for the connection to be valid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two two-port networks are connected in series. The Z-parameters of network $N_a$ are: $$ Z_a = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 12 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ and the Z-parameters of network $N_b$ are: $$ Z_b = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ Find the Z-parameters of the equivalent two-port. Is the equivalent network reciprocal? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Consider two two-port networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ with their input ports connected in series and output ports connected in series. Let $I_1$ and $I_2$ be the currents entering Port 1 and Port 2 of both networks (same currents due to series connection). The Z-parameter equations for $N_a$ are: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1a} &= Z_{11a} I_1 + Z_{12a} I_2 \\ V_{2a} &= Z_{21a} I_1 + Z_{22a} I_2 \end{aligned} $$ For $N_b$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1b} &= Z_{11b} I_1 + Z_{12b} I_2 \\ V_{2b} &= Z_{21b} I_1 + Z_{22b} I_2 \end{aligned} $$ By KVL at the input and output loops: $$ V_1 = V_{1a} + V_{1b}, \quad V_2 = V_{2a} + V_{2b} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= (Z_{11a} + Z_{11b})I_1 + (Z_{12a} + Z_{12b})I_2 \\ V_2 &= (Z_{21a} + Z_{21b})I_1 + (Z_{22a} + Z_{22b})I_2 \end{aligned} $$ Comparing with the standard Z-form: $$ \boxed{Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b} $$ **Condition for validity (Bruton test):** The series connection is valid only if the two ports of the resulting network remain **distinct and accessible**, i.e., the same current $I_1$ enters the input of both $N_a$ and $N_b$, and the same $I_2$ enters the output of both. If any internal connection forces a different current distribution, the connection is invalid and the matrix addition does not apply. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $$ Z_a = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 12 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega, \quad Z_b = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ Applying the series combination formula $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$: $$ Z_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 10+8 & 4+2 \\ 4+2 & 12+6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 6 \\ 6 & 18 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ **Checking reciprocity:** A two-port network is reciprocal if and only if $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. Here: $$ Z_{12,eq} = 6\ \Omega = Z_{21,eq} $$ Hence, the equivalent network is **reciprocal**. This is expected, because the sum of two reciprocal matrices is always reciprocal. **Final Answer:** $$ Z_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 6 \\ 6 & 18 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ The network is reciprocal and also symmetric (since $Z_{11,eq} = Z_{22,eq} = 18\ \Omega$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Derive the relationship for finding the Y-parameters of a two-port network formed by the **parallel connection of two two-port networks** $N_a$ and $N_b$. Mention the condition for the connection to be valid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two two-port networks are connected in parallel. The Y-parameters of $N_a$ are: $$ Y_a = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S} $$ and the Y-parameters of $N_b$ are: $$ Y_b = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S} $$ Find the Y-parameters of the equivalent two-port. Compute the corresponding Z-parameters and verify whether the combined network is reciprocal. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Let the two networks $N_a$ and $N_b$ be connected in parallel at both ports. The input port voltage $V_1$ is common to both, and the output port voltage $V_2$ is common to both. The currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ entering the combined port split into components for each sub-network: $$ I_1 = I_{1a} + I_{1b}, \quad I_2 = I_{2a} + I_{2b} $$ Y-parameter equations for $N_a$: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{1a} &= Y_{11a} V_1 + Y_{12a} V_2 \\ I_{2a} &= Y_{21a} V_1 + Y_{22a} V_2 \end{aligned} $$ For $N_b$: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{1b} &= Y_{11b} V_1 + Y_{12b} V_2 \\ I_{2b} &= Y_{21b} V_1 + Y_{22b} V_2 \end{aligned} $$ Adding: $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= (Y_{11a} + Y_{11b})V_1 + (Y_{12a} + Y_{12b})V_2 \\ I_2 &= (Y_{21a} + Y_{21b})V_1 + (Y_{22a} + Y_{22b})V_2 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b} $$ **Validity condition:** The parallel connection is valid only if the two ports of the resulting network remain distinct (Bruton test). If an unintended internal loop forms between the paralleled sub-networks such that the same voltage condition is violated, the connection is invalid. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $$ Y_a = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S}, \quad Y_b = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S} $$ Apply parallel combination: $$ Y_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 5+3 & -2+(-1) \\ -2+(-1) & 4+6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & -3 \\ -3 & 10 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S} $$ **Convert Y to Z using $Z_{eq} = Y_{eq}^{-1}$:** Determinant: $$ \Delta Y = (8)(10) - (-3)(-3) = 80 - 9 = 71\ \text{S}^2 $$ Inverse: $$ Z_{eq} = \frac{1}{71} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 3 \\ 3 & 8 \end{bmatrix} $$ So: $$ Z_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/71 & 3/71 \\ 3/71 & 8/71 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.1408 & 0.0423 \\ 0.0423 & 0.1127 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ **Check reciprocity:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 3/71\ \Omega$ → **The combined network is reciprocal.** **Final Answer:** $$ Y_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & -3 \\ -3 & 10 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{S}, \quad Z_{eq} = \frac{1}{71}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 3 \\ 3 & 8 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega $$ The combined network is reciprocal because $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (and equivalently $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong parameter set for the connection type:** Using Y-parameters for series or Z-parameters for parallel is a fundamental conceptual error. Marks deducted: 2–3. > 2. **Forgetting to convert h- or T-parameters to Z or Y first:** If the network is given in h-parameters, you **must** convert to Z-parameters using $Z_{11} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_{22}}$, $Z_{12} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}$, $Z_{21} = \frac{-h_{21}}{h_{22}}$, $Z_{22} = \frac{1}{h_{22}}$ before adding. Marks deducted: 2. > 3. **Not mentioning the Bruton validity test:** KTU specifically tests this. A full-marks answer must state the condition. Marks deducted: 1–2. > 4. **Sign errors in current direction:** Always assume currents enter the ports (+ direction). If the diagram shows the opposite, the answer is sign-flipped. Marks deducted: 1 per sub-part. > 5. **Skipping the reciprocity check:** When the question asks "is it reciprocal?", you must explicitly state $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ and conclude. Marks deducted: 1. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Series and Parallel Connections of Two-Port Networks** - ✅ A **two-port network** has two accessible ports with terminal variables $(V_1, I_1)$ at Port 1 and $(V_2, I_2)$ at Port 2. - ✅ The **standard current convention** is that $I_1$ and $I_2$ *enter* the positive terminals of their respective ports. - ✅ The **six standard parameter sets** are Z, Y, h, g, T (ABCD), and T' (inverse transmission). They are interrelated via matrix inverses and determinants. - ✅ **Series connection** uses Z-parameters: $Z_{eq} = Z_a + Z_b$ (direct matrix sum). - ✅ **Parallel connection** uses Y-parameters: $Y_{eq} = Y_a + Y_b$ (direct matrix sum). - ✅ The **Bruton validity test** must be satisfied: ports must remain distinct and accessible after interconnection, otherwise matrix addition is invalid. - ✅ For **series** connection: $I_1$ and $I_2$ are common to both sub-networks; voltages add (KVL). - ✅ For **parallel** connection: $V_1$ and $V_2$ are common to both sub-networks; currents add (KCL). - ✅ **Reciprocity** is preserved under both series and parallel combination: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$) holds if both sub-networks are individually reciprocal. - ✅ **Symmetry** is preserved if both sub-networks are individually symmetric. - ✅ If the network is given in **h-parameters**, convert to Z first using: - $Z_{11} = \Delta h / h_{22}$ - $Z_{12} = h_{12} / h_{22}$ - $Z_{21} = -h_{21} / h_{22}$ - $Z_{22} = 1 / h_{22}$ - ✅ If the network is given in **T-parameters** (ABCD), use $Z_{11} = A/C$, $Z_{12} = \Delta T / C$, $Z_{21} = 1/C$, $Z_{22} = D/C$. - ✅ KTU exam marks distribution: 7 marks for derivation, 7 marks for numerical — always show full working, even in the derivation. - ✅ The combined Z (or Y) matrix preserves the units of the original parameters ($\Omega$ for Z, S for Y). - ✅ **Cascade (chain) connection is different** — it uses T-parameters via matrix multiplication, not series/parallel addition. Don't confuse the three. ---

topics.title: Passive Filters - Filter Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Passive Filters - Filter Fundamentals # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Electrical Filter? An **electrical filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to pass signals within a specified range of frequencies (the **passband**) while attenuating (rejecting) signals outside this range (the **stopband**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"A filter is a reactive network that passes a specified band of frequencies while attenuating all frequencies outside this band."* In the **frequency domain**, a filter's behavior is fully described by its **transfer function** $H(j\omega)$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)} = \vert H(j\omega) \vert \, \angle \, \phi(\omega) $$ where: - $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ → **Magnitude response** (gain / attenuation) - $\phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega)$ → **Phase response** (delay / distortion) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strainer in a Pipe" Imagine pouring a mixture of **coarse sand and fine clay** into a kitchen strainer held over a bucket. The strainer physically *passes* the fine clay (small particles) into the bucket below, while *rejecting* (blocking) the coarse sand (large particles). A **filter** does exactly the same thing — but with **electrical signals instead of particles, and frequency instead of physical size**: | Kitchen Strainer Analogy | Electrical Filter Equivalent | |---|---| | Mixture poured in | Input signal (sum of many frequencies) | | Fine clay passes through | Frequencies inside the **passband** | | Coarse sand held back | Frequencies inside the **stopband** | | Mesh size (small/large holes) | **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ | | Type of strainer (sieve, colander…) | Filter **type** (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** > Passive filters use only **resistors (R), inductors (L), and capacitors (C)**. They require **no external power supply**, dissipate signal energy, and cannot provide gain (gain $\le 1$ always). ## 1.3 Why Do We Need Filters? Filters are fundamental building blocks in: - **Communication systems** → selecting a desired radio channel while rejecting adjacent ones. - **Audio engineering** → separating bass (woofer) from treble (tweeter) signals via crossover networks. - **Power systems** → suppressing harmonics produced by nonlinear loads (rectifiers, SMPS). - **Instrumentation** → removing **50 Hz power-line hum** from biomedical signals (ECG, EEG). - **Data converters** → anti-aliasing (before ADC) and reconstruction (after DAC). ## 1.4 Classification of Filters ### A. By Physical Construction | Class | Components | Requires Power? | Provides Gain? | |---|---|---|---| | **Passive Filter** | R, L, C only | **No** | **No** (Loss only) | | **Active Filter** | R, L, C + Op-Amp / Transistor | **Yes (Vcc / Vee)** | **Yes** | ### B. By Frequency-Selectivity Function | Filter Type | Symbol | Passes | Rejects | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | 🔻 | $0 \to f_c$ | $f > f_c$ | Audio bass, anti-aliasing | | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | 🔺 | $f > f_c$ | $0 \to f_c$ | DC blocker, treble boost | | **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | ⛰️ | $f_L \to f_H$ | Outside band | Radio tuner, channel select | | **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** / Notch | 🏞️ | Outside band | $f_L \to f_H$ | 50 Hz hum rejection | ### C. By Approximation (Response Shape) - **Butterworth** → Maximally flat magnitude in passband. - **Chebyshev** → Sharper cutoff, but has passband ripple. - **Bessel** → Maximally flat group delay (linear phase). - **Elliptic (Cauer)** → Sharpest cutoff with ripple in both bands. ## 1.5 The Concept of Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$)** is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its maximum passband value. In decibels, this corresponds to a drop of: $$ 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -3.0103 \,\text{dB} \approx \mathbf{-3\,dB} $$ Hence $f_c$ is universally called the **"-3 dB frequency"**, **"half-power frequency"**, or **"corner frequency"**. At $f_c$, the output **power** is exactly half the maximum (since $P \propto V^2$, and $V$ falls by $1/\sqrt{2}$). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Filter Response A practical filter's magnitude response $|\,H(j\omega)\,|$ is divided into three distinct regions. Consider an LPF as a working example: ``` |H(jω)| 1.0 ┤━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ ← PASSBAND (signal passes, |H| ≈ 1) │ ┃ 0.707┤- - - - - - -╂━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ -3 dB line │ ┃ ╲ ← TRANSITION BAND (signal attenuated) 0 ┤ ┃ ╲___________ └─────────────┸────────────────────── ω ωc ``` | Region | Frequency Range | Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Passband** | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx 1$ (0 dB) | | **Transition Band** | $\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_s$ | Gradual roll-off from 0 dB → stopband level | | **Stopband** | $\omega \ge \omega_s$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \le A_{min}$ (highly attenuated) | ## 2.2 Filter Order and Roll-Off Rate > [!IMPORTANT] > **Filter Order ($N$)** = the highest power of $s$ (or $\omega$) in the denominator of the transfer function $H(s)$. The **roll-off rate** (asymptotic attenuation slope beyond $\omega_c$) is: $$ \text{Roll-off} = 20 \cdot N \quad \text{dB/decade} \quad = \quad 6 \cdot N \quad \text{dB/octave} $$ | Order $N$ | Roll-off (dB/decade) | Roll-off (dB/octave) | Poles | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 20 | 6 | 1 | | 2 | 40 | 12 | 2 | | 3 | 60 | 18 | 3 | | 4 | 80 | 24 | 4 | | $N$ | $20N$ | $6N$ | $N$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** A higher-order filter gives a *sharper* transition from passband to stopband, but at the cost of: > - More components (cost ↑) > - More phase distortion (group delay non-linearity ↑) > - Greater sensitivity to component tolerances (Q-factor of poles ↑) ## 2.3 Butterworth Filter Approximation — The "Gold Standard" The **Butterworth filter** is the most commonly designed filter in KTU examinations because of its **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband. ### Magnitude-Squared Function For a low-pass Butterworth filter of order $N$ with cutoff $\omega_c$: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$ ### Key Properties 1. **At DC** ($\omega = 0$): $\vert H \vert^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \vert H \vert = 1$ (unity gain). 2. **At cutoff** ($\omega = \omega_c$): $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ($-3$ dB **for all $N$**). 3. **At infinity** ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert H \vert^2 \to 0$ (full attenuation). 4. **Flatness**: The first $(2N-1)$ derivatives of $\vert H \vert^2$ are zero at $\omega = 0$, ensuring maximum passband flatness. ### First Few Orders Explicitly | Order $N$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2$ | Roll-off (dB/dec) | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^2}$ | 20 | | 2 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^4}$ | 40 | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^6}$ | 60 | | $N$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$ | $20N$ | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bookmark this table — every KTU exam question on filters is built from these formulas.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = V_o/V_i$ | dimensionless | complex ratio | | 2 | Magnitude (dB) | $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$ | dB | negative ⇒ attenuation | | 3 | Phase | $\phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega)$ | radians or degrees | | | 4 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF) RC | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | $-3$ dB point | | 5 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF) RL | $f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$ | Hz | $-3$ dB point | | 6 | Roll-off rate | $20N$ dB/decade | dB/dec | $N$ = order | | 7 | Butterworth $\vert H \vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$ | — | unitless | | 8 | Quality factor $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_c}{BW}$ | — | BPF only | | 9 | Bandwidth $BW$ | $f_H - f_L$ | Hz | BPF | | 10 | Pole magnitude Butterworth | $\omega_k = \omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$ | rad/s | $k = 1,2,\dots,N$ | | 11 | Group delay | $\tau_g = -\dfrac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega}$ | seconds | constant ⇒ linear phase | | 12 | Phase delay | $\tau_p = -\dfrac{\phi(\omega)}{\omega}$ | seconds | | > **Note on Table Notation:** In every row above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (magnitude). The vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to prevent conflicts with markdown table pipes. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Filter Fundamentals | Engineering Field | Application | Filter Type Used | |---|---|---| | Power Quality | Suppression of harmonic currents from VFDs | Passive L-C tuned LPF | | Audio Crossovers | Splitting audio into woofer/tweeter feeds | 2nd-order LPF + HPF | | RF Communications | Channel selection in superheterodyne receivers | Multi-pole BPF | | Biomedical | Removing 50 Hz mains interference from ECG | Notch (BSF) | | DAC Reconstruction | Smoothing staircase output of DAC | LPF (anti-image) | | ADC Anti-Aliasing | Band-limiting input before sampling | LPF | ## 2.6 Why "Passive" Filters Are First in the KTU Syllabus Passive filters are studied **first** because: 1. They are built from R, L, C — the same components already mastered in earlier modules. 2. They form the **theoretical foundation** for active filter design (op-amp circuits later replace bulky inductors with simulated inductance / FDNR techniques). 3. They are still the **preferred solution** at high power levels (kW range) where active filters cannot operate. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: Cutoff Frequency of First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter **Circuit:** Series R and C, output taken across the capacitor. ``` R C Vin ──/\/\/──┬──┤├──┬──── Vout │ │ ─┴─ ─┴─ GND GND ``` ### Step 1: Apply Voltage Divider Rule The impedance of the resistor is $Z_R = R$ and the capacitor is $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$. $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute and Simplify $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ ### Step 3: Express Magnitude $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the $-3$ dB Definition Set $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$ Square both sides: $$ 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 $$ $$ (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 $$ $$ \omega_c RC = 1 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\;} $$ ### Step 5: Verify Limiting Cases (Sanity Check) - **At $\omega = 0$ (DC):** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+0}} = 1$ ✓ (capacitor is open-circuit, full DC passes). - **At $\omega \to \infty$:** $\vert H \vert \to \dfrac{1}{\infty} = 0$ ✓ (capacitor is short-circuit, output is zero). - **At $\omega = \omega_c$:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = -3$ dB ✓. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Roll-Off Rate of $N$-th Order Butterworth LPF ### Step 1: Write the Magnitude-Squared Function $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$ ### Step 2: Take the Logarithm (to convert to dB) $$ A_{dB}(\omega) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}\right] $$ ### Step 3: Approximation at High Frequencies ($\omega \gg \omega_c$) For $\omega \gg \omega_c$, the term $(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N} \gg 1$, so: $$ A_{dB}(\omega) \approx -10 \log_{10}\!\left[(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}\right] = -10 \cdot 2N \cdot \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c) $$ $$ \boxed{\;A_{dB}(\omega) \approx -20N \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c) \quad \text{for } \omega \gg \omega_c\;} $$ ### Step 4: Interpret as Slope When $\omega$ increases by a factor of 10 (one **decade**), $\log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c)$ increases by 1, so $A_{dB}$ changes by $-20N$ dB. **Conclusion:** Roll-off = $-20N$ dB/decade = $-6N$ dB/octave. --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: Phase Response of First-Order RC LPF From Step 2 of Derivation #1: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} = \frac{1}{1 + j(\omega/\omega_c)} $$ Phase: $$ \phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right) $$ | $\omega$ | $\phi(\omega)$ | |---|---| | 0 | $0°$ | | $\omega_c$ | $-45°$ | | $10\,\omega_c$ | $-84.3°$ | | $\infty$ | $-90°$ | The phase decreases monotonically from $0°$ to $-90°$ — characteristic of a single-pole system. --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4: Butterworth Pole Locations Poles of $H(s)H(-s)$ are found from: $$ 1 + \left(\frac{s}{j\omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad s^{2N} = (-1)^{N+1}\,\omega_c^{2N} $$ The $2N$ roots lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the $s$-plane, **symmetric about both axes**, and *none on the imaginary axis* (for stability of a passive network, the $N$ left-half-plane roots are selected). $$ \boxed{\;s_k = \omega_c \cdot \exp\!\left[j\,\frac{\pi(2k + N - 1)}{2N}\right], \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N\;} $$ For stability, we retain only poles with $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$, i.e., the left-half-plane roots. ### Example: $N = 3$ (3rd-Order Butterworth) $$ s_k = \omega_c \cdot \exp\!\left[j\,\frac{\pi(2k + 2)}{6}\right], \quad k = 1, \ldots, 6 $$ | $k$ | Angle (°) | $s_k$ (in units of $\omega_c$) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 60° | $\omega_c(-0.5 + j0.866)$ | | 2 | 120° | $\omega_c(-1 + j0)$ | | 3 | 180° | $\omega_c(-0.5 - j0.866)$ | | 4 | 240° | $\omega_c(0.5 - j0.866)$ | | 5 | 300° | $\omega_c(1 - j0)$ | | 6 | 360° | $\omega_c(0.5 + j0.866)$ | **LHP poles** (stable): $k = 1, 2, 3$ → choose these. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Filter Types | Property | Butterworth | Chebyshev | Bessel | Elliptic | |---|---|---|---|---| | Passband | Maximally flat | Equi-ripple | Flat | Equi-ripple | | Stopband | Monotonic | Monotonic | Monotonic | Equi-ripple | | Phase / Group delay | Moderate non-linearity | Most non-linear | **Maximally linear** | Highly non-linear | | Roll-off sharpness (same $N$) | Moderate | **Sharpest** | Gentle | **Sharpest of all** | | Pole locations | Circle | Ellipse | — | — | | KTU exam prevalence | **Highest** | Medium | Low | Lowest | | Best use | General purpose | Sharp cutoff needed | Pulse / data signals | Ultra-sharp cutoff | --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style **Problem:** A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter has cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz. Find the attenuation (in dB) at $f = 10$ kHz. ### Solution **Step 1:** Identify parameters: $N = 2$, $f = 10 f_c$. **Step 2:** Apply Butterworth magnitude formula: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^4} = \frac{1}{1 + (10)^4} = \frac{1}{10001} $$ **Step 3:** Compute magnitude: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10001}} \approx \frac{1}{100.005} \approx 0.0099995 $$ **Step 4:** Convert to dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0099995) = -20 \log_{10}(100.005) \approx -40.0009 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 5:** Cross-check using the roll-off rule: Since $f = 10 f_c$ is **one decade** above $f_c$, the attenuation should be approximately: $$ \text{Roll-off} = -20 \cdot N = -20 \cdot 2 = -40 \text{ dB/decade} $$ ✅ **Excellent agreement!** The exact answer $-40.0009$ dB matches the asymptotic $-40$ dB closely (small error due to the $-3$ dB offset at $f_c$). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with `node`. - No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as node names. - Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting. --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Filter System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE
