Why is it called the "-3 dB Frequency"? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **cutoff frequency** ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$) of a filter is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function falls to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately **70.7 %**) of its maximum passband value. It is called the **"-3 dB frequency"** because converting this voltage ratio to decibels yields: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}(2) \approx -3.0103 \text{ dB} \approx -3 \text{ dB} $$ It is also called the **half-power frequency** because at $f_c$, the output power is exactly one-half the maximum passband power. --- ### Question 2: State the four main types of filters based on frequency selectivity. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | # | Filter Type | Passes | Rejects | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Frequencies from 0 to $f_c$ | Frequencies above $f_c$ | | 2 | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | Frequencies above $f_c$ | Frequencies from 0 to $f_c$ | | 3 | **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | Frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$ | Frequencies outside this band | | 4 | **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** / Notch | Frequencies outside $(f_L, f_H)$ | Frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$ | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order RC low-pass filter. Find its cutoff frequency in terms of $R$ and $C$. Show that the magnitude rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 3rd-order Butterworth low-pass filter has a cutoff frequency of $f_c = 5$ kHz. Calculate: **(i)** the magnitude of the transfer function (in dB) at $f = 50$ kHz, **(ii)** the asymptotic roll-off rate. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** **(a)** Explain the four classical filter approximations (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, Elliptic) with reference to their magnitude response, phase linearity, and roll-off sharpness. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The magnitude-squared function of a low-pass filter is given by: $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/2000\pi)^8} $$ Identify the filter type, find its order, cutoff frequency, and the attenuation in dB at $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s. **(7 Marks)** --- ## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION A ### Solution to A(a) **Step 1 — Circuit Analysis:** Consider the series RC network with output across the capacitor. The impedances are $Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$. **Step 2 — Voltage Divider:** $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} = \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ **Step 3 — Cutoff Frequency:** Set $\omega_c RC = 1$: $$ \boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}, \qquad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}} $$ **Step 4 — Magnitude:** $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ **Step 5 — Roll-Off at High Frequency:** For $\omega \gg \omega_c$: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC} $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(\omega RC) $$ When $\omega$ increases by factor 10 (one decade), $A_{dB}$ drops by $20$ dB. ∎ --- ### Solution to A(b) **Given:** $N = 3$, $f_c = 5$ kHz, $f = 50$ kHz → ratio $= 10$. **(i) Magnitude at 50 kHz:** $$ \bigl| H(j2\pi \cdot 50000) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (10)^{2 \cdot 3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^6} \approx 1 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \bigl| H \bigr| \approx \sqrt{10^{-6}} = 10^{-3} $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = -60 \text{ dB} $$ **(ii) Roll-off Rate:** $$ \text{Roll-off} = 20 N = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ dB/decade} $$ **Cross-check:** A 10× (one-decade) increase above $f_c$ yields exactly $60$ dB drop for a 3rd-order Butterworth — consistent with the $-60$ dB found above. ✅ --- ## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION B ### Solution to B(a) | Property | Butterworth | Chebyshev | Bessel | Elliptic | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Passband** | Maximally flat (no ripple) | Equi-ripple | Flat | Equi-ripple | | **Stopband** | Monotonic | Monotonic | Monotonic | Equi-ripple | | **Phase linearity** | Moderate | Poor | **Best** | Poor | | **Roll-off sharpness (same $N$)** | Moderate | Very sharp | Gentle | **Sharpest** | | **Pole locations** | Circle of radius $\omega_c$ | Ellipse | — | — | | **Best for** | General purpose | Sharp cutoff | Pulse/data | Ultra-sharp cutoff | **Exam Tip:** Mention the defining mathematical feature of each — e.g., Butterworth's maximally flat magnitude comes from the first $(2N-1)$ derivatives being zero at $\omega = 0$. --- ### Solution to B(b) **Given:** $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/2000\pi)^8} $$ **Step 1 — Compare with standard Butterworth form:** $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}} $$ Matching exponents: $\boxed{2N = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 4}$. **Step 2 — Identify filter type:** Magnitude squared form matches **Butterworth** with $N = 4$. **Step 3 — Cutoff frequency:** $$ \omega_c = 2000\pi \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{2000\pi}{2\pi} = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 4 — Attenuation at $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s:** Ratio: $\omega/\omega_c = \dfrac{4000\pi}{2000\pi} = 2$. $$ \vert H \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (2)^8} = \frac{1}{1 + 256} = \frac{1}{257} $$ $$ \vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{257}} \approx \frac{1}{16.031} \approx 0.0624 $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0624) \approx -24.1 \text{ dB} $$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing cutoff frequency with $-3$ dB point definitions.** The $-3$ dB definition applies to *Butterworth and Bessel* filters. For Chebyshev filters, the "cutoff" is sometimes taken at the passband-edge ripple (e.g., $-0.5$ dB or $-1$ dB), not $-3$ dB. **Read the question carefully.** > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2N$.** The exponent in the Butterworth magnitude-squared function is $2N$, NOT $N$. Students writing $\dfrac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^N}$ lose full marks. > > 3. **Computing magnitude and phase separately but losing magnitude in dB conversion.** Always compute $\vert H \vert$ first, THEN convert: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H \vert$. > > 4. **Confusing rad/s with Hz in cutoff frequency.** If the question gives $f_c$ in Hz, convert to $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ rad/s **before** plugging into formulas. > > 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units (Hz, rad/s, dB). > > 6. **Active vs Passive confusion.** "Filter Fundamentals" in Module 4 means **passive only** unless explicitly stated. Don't introduce op-amps. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:** - ☐ A **filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network. It does NOT add energy — only redistributes or attenuates it. - ☐ **Passive filters** use only $R$, $L$, $C$. No external power, no gain, always $\vert H \vert \le 1$. - ☐ **Four filter types**: LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF (Notch). - ☐ **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is the $-3$ dB point: $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$, $A_{dB} = -3$ dB. - ☐ For a 1st-order RC LPF: $\;f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. For RL LPF: $\;f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$. - ☐ **Filter order $N$** = highest power of $s$ in denominator. - ☐ **Roll-off rate** $= 20N$ dB/decade $= 6N$ dB/octave. - ☐ **Butterworth** magnitude-squared: $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$. - ☐ Butterworth has **maximally flat** passband (first $2N-1$ derivatives zero at $\omega = 0$). - ☐ Butterworth poles lie on a **circle of radius $\omega_c$** in the $s$-plane, symmetric about the real axis; only LHP poles are chosen. - ☐ **Chebyshev** = sharper roll-off but passband ripple; **Bessel** = best phase linearity (pulse preservation); **Elliptic** = sharpest cutoff but ripple in both bands. - ☐ **Magnitude in dB:** $\;A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$. **Phase:** $\;\phi = \angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_c)$ for 1st-order LPF. - ☐ At $f = f_c$: phase = $-45°$ for any 1st-order LPF. - ☐ Asymptotic magnitude at high $\omega$: $\;A_{dB} \approx -20N \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c)$. - ☐ **KTU favourite questions**: (i) Derive cutoff frequency of RC LPF/HPF. (ii) Compute attenuation in dB at given frequency for Butterworth. (iii) Compare filter approximations. (iv) Identify filter type, order, and cutoff from given $\vert H \vert^2$.

topics.title: Classification of Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FILTER (Network Theory Definition):**
> A **filter** is a two-port frequency-selective network designed to pass signals within a specified frequency range (the **passband**) with minimal attenuation, while attenuating signals outside this range (the **stopband**) to a specified level. Mathematically, it is a network whose **transfer function $H(j\omega)$** has a magnitude response that is a function of frequency $\omega$ in a prescribed manner.

In the context of **KTU PEEBT631 – Module 4**, filters are studied from the perspective of classical **passive network synthesis**, employing inductors (L) and capacitors (C). The foundation rests on **image parameter method** and the **insertion loss method**.

## 1.2 Classification Hierarchy

Filters are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|------------|
| **Frequency Response** | Low Pass (LPF), High Pass (HPF), Band Pass (BPF), Band Stop (BSF) |
| **Construction / Elements** | Passive (LC, RC, RL), Active (Op-amp based), Digital |
| **Magnitude Response Shape** | Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic (Cauer), Bessel (Thompson) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a filter as a **selective bouncer at a nightclub**.
> - The **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a bouncer who allows only **bass-heavy, slow-music lovers** (low-frequency signals) inside and rejects the high-energy dancers (high-frequency signals).
> - The **High Pass Filter (HPF)** is the opposite — only treble and high-frequency "energy" enters.
> - The **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a bouncer at a **themed retro night** — only people in costumes matching a specific style (a band of frequencies between $f_1$ and $f_2$) are admitted.
> - The **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** is a bouncer who rejects **only one specific group** (say, people wearing red shirts between a particular range) — useful for notch-out a particular interference.

In electronics, the same selective discrimination happens, but using the **reactance** properties of **inductors ($X_L = \omega L$)** and **capacitors ($X_C = 1/\omega C$)**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Passband ($\omega_p$):** Frequency range where signal is transmitted with attenuation $\leq A_{max}$ (typically 3 dB).
> - **Stopband ($\omega_s$):** Frequency range where signal is attenuated by at least $A_{min}$ (typically 30 dB or more).
> - **Cut-off Frequency ($\omega_c$ or $f_c$):** The boundary frequency where signal power drops to **half** (-3 dB point). At $\omega = \omega_c$, $\vert H(j\omega_c)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
> - **Roll-off Rate:** The steepness of attenuation outside the passband, measured in **dB/decade** or **dB/octave**. For an $n^{th}$-order filter, roll-off = $20n$ dB/decade.
> - **Order of Filter ($n$):** The number of energy-storing elements (L or C) in the filter; determines the sharpness of the response.
> - **Characteristic Impedance ($Z_0$ or $Z_{0T}$, $Z_{0\pi}$):** The iterative impedance of a symmetric T or π network.
> - **Prototype Filter:** A normalized low-pass filter with $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s and $R_0 = 1\;\Omega$, later scaled to actual values.

---

## 1.5 Classification Based on Frequency Response (Most Important for KTU)

### (a) Low Pass Filter (LPF)
Passes $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$; attenuates $\omega > \omega_c$.

$$
\vert H(j\omega)\vert = 
\begin{cases}
\approx 1, & 0 \le \omega \le \omega_c \\
\approx 0, & \omega > \omega_c
\end{cases}
$$

### (b) High Pass Filter (HPF)
Attenuates $0 \le \omega < \omega_c$; passes $\omega \ge \omega_c$.

### (c) Band Pass Filter (BPF)
Passes only $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$ (between two cut-offs); rejects everything else.

**Bandwidth:** $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$, **Center frequency:** $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$

### (d) Band Stop / Band Elimination Filter (BSF / BEF)
Passes all frequencies EXCEPT those in $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$. (Also called **Notch Filter** when BW is very narrow.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Filter Operation — The Physics Behind Filtering

### How an LPF Works (Physical Insight)

The behavior arises purely from **frequency-dependent reactance**:

- For an **inductor**, $X_L = \omega L$:
  - At **low** $\omega \to X_L \to 0$ → Inductor behaves like a **short circuit** (signal passes).
  - At **high** $\omega \to X_L \to \infty$ → Inductor behaves like an **open circuit** (signal blocked).

- For a **capacitor**, $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$:
  - At **low** $\omega \to X_C \to \infty$ → Capacitor behaves like an **open circuit** (signal blocked).
  - At **high** $\omega \to X_C \to 0$ → Capacitor behaves like a **short circuit** (signal passes to ground).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** By combining inductors (in series) and capacitors (in shunt to ground), we exploit their complementary behavior at different frequencies. An **L-section LPF** uses:
> - Series **L** → passes low frequencies, blocks high frequencies.
> - Shunt **C** → shorts high frequencies to ground, blocks low frequencies.

This is the foundational principle behind ALL classical filter design.

---

## 2.2 Prototype Filter Sections (Image Parameter Approach)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks derivations and the design of prototype T and π sections.** Memorize these structures.

### 2.2.1 T-Section Prototype LPF

A symmetric T-section with series arms $\frac{Z_1}{2}$ and shunt arm $Z_2$:

**Series arm:** $\frac{Z_1}{2} = \frac{L}{2}$

**Shunt arm:** $Z_2 = C$

### 2.2.2 π-Section Prototype LPF

**Series arm:** $Z_1 = L$

**Shunt arm:** $2Z_2 = 2C$

### 2.2.3 Characteristic Impedance — T Section

$$
Z_{0T} = \sqrt{\frac{Z_1^2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2}
$$

### 2.2.4 Characteristic Impedance — π Section

$$
Z_{0\pi} = \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{\sqrt{\frac{Z_1^2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2}} = \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_{0T}}
$$

### 2.2.5 Propagation Constant ($\gamma$)

$$
\gamma = \alpha + j\beta
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **Attenuation constant** (Np/m or dB/m)
- $\beta$ = **Phase constant** (rad/m)

For T-section:

$$
\cosh(\gamma) = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{2 Z_2}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — KEEP THIS FOR REVISION**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Inductor Reactance | $X_L = \omega L$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Capacitor Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | Resonant Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | LPF Cut-off (RC) | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | HPF Cut-off (RC) | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | BPF Bandwidth | $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ | rad/s |
| 7 | BPF Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Roll-off (LPF/HPF) | $20n$ dB/decade | $n$ = filter order |
| 9 | Butterworth $\vert H \vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$ | Maximally flat |
| 10 | Chebyshev $\vert H \vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 T_n^2(\omega/\omega_c)}$ | Equal ripple |
| 11 | Prototype L (LPF) | $L = \dfrac{R_0}{\omega_c}$ | Henry (H) |
| 12 | Prototype C (LPF) | $C = \dfrac{1}{R_0 \omega_c}$ | Farad (F) |
| 13 | Prototype L (HPF) | $L = \dfrac{R_0}{\omega_c}$ | Henry (H) |
| 14 | Prototype C (HPF) | $C = \dfrac{1}{R_0 \omega_c}$ | Farad (F) |
| 15 | $Z_{0T}$ of T-section | $\sqrt{Z_1^2/4 + Z_1 Z_2}$ | Ohms |
| 16 | $Z_{0\pi}$ of π-section | $Z_1 Z_2 / Z_{0T}$ | Ohms |
| 17 | $m$-derived L (series) | $\dfrac{1-m^2}{m} L$ | Henry (H) |
| 18 | $m$-derived C (shunt) | $\dfrac{m}{1-m^2} C$ | Farad (F) |
| 19 | Frequency Transformation (LPF→HPF) | $s \rightarrow \dfrac{\omega_c}{s}$ | Variable substitution |
| 20 | Frequency Transformation (LPF→BPF) | $s \rightarrow \dfrac{s^2+\omega_0^2}{BW\cdot s}$ | Band parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Filter design is foundational in:
> - **Audio systems:** Crossovers route bass to woofers, treble to tweeters.
> - **Communication:** Channel selection in radios (BPF).
> - **Power systems:** Harmonic elimination using BPF/BSF.
> - **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning (BSF for 50 Hz line noise).

---

## 2.4 Classification Based on Magnitude Response Approximation

### 2.4.1 Butterworth Filter (Maximally Flat)
- Flattest passband response (no ripples).
- $\vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2n}}$
- Trade-off: Slower transition from passband to stopband.

### 2.4.2 Chebyshev Filter (Type I & II)
- **Type I:** Ripple in passband, monotonic stopband.
- **Type II:** Flat passband, ripple in stopband.
- Faster transition than Butterworth for the same order.

### 2.4.3 Elliptic / Cauer Filter
- Ripple in BOTH passband and stopband.
- **Sharpest transition** for a given order.

### 2.4.4 Bessel / Thompson Filter
- Maximally **linear phase** response → best **pulse** preservation.
- Poorest magnitude selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Impedance of a Symmetric T-Section

**Starting Network:** Symmetric T-network with series arm $Z_1/2$ – shunt arm $Z_2$ – series arm $Z_1/2$.

By the **image parameter definition**, characteristic impedance $Z_{0T}$ is the impedance measured at one port when the other port is terminated in $Z_{0T}$ itself.

Apply a voltage $V_1$ at port 1 and terminate port 2 with $Z_{0T}$. The input impedance equals $Z_{0T}$.

Using the iterative formula for symmetric T:

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{Z_1}{2} + \frac{Z_2 \cdot \left(\frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right)}{Z_2 + \frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}}
$$

For the definition of iterative impedance: $Z_{in} = Z_{0T}$.

$$
Z_{0T} = \frac{Z_1}{2} + \frac{Z_2\left(\frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right)}{Z_2 + \frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}}
$$

Multiply both sides by $\left(Z_2 + \frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right)$:

$$
Z_{0T}\left(Z_2 + \frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right) = \frac{Z_1}{2}\left(Z_2 + \frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right) + Z_2\left(\frac{Z_1}{2} + Z_{0T}\right)
$$

Expanding LHS:

$$
Z_{0T}Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_{0T}}{2} + Z_{0T}^2 = \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{2} + \frac{Z_1^2}{4} + \frac{Z_1 Z_{0T}}{2} + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{2} + Z_2 Z_{0T}
$$

The term $\frac{Z_1 Z_{0T}}{2}$ cancels from both sides:

$$
Z_{0T}Z_2 + Z_{0T}^2 = \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{2} + \frac{Z_1^2}{4} + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{2}
$$

$$
Z_{0T}^2 = \frac{Z_1^2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{0T} = \sqrt{\frac{Z_1^2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2}}
$$

This is the **standard textbook formula** that the KTU examiner expects verbatim.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cut-off Frequency of a Prototype LPF

For a prototype **T-section LPF** with $Z_1 = j\omega L$ (inductor) and $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ (capacitor):

$$
Z_1 Z_2 = (j\omega L)\left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = \frac{L}{C}
$$

$$
\frac{Z_1^2}{4} = \frac{(j\omega L)^2}{4} = -\frac{\omega^2 L^2}{4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z_{0T}^2 = -\frac{\omega^2 L^2}{4} + \frac{L}{C}
$$

At the **cut-off frequency** $\omega_c$, the propagation constant $\gamma = 0$ (boundary between passband and stopband). This occurs when $Z_{0T}^2$ transitions from real positive to negative (or vice-versa).

For $\omega \le \omega_c$ (passband): $Z_{0T}^2$ is real and positive.
For $\omega > \omega_c$ (stopband): $Z_{0T}^2$ is negative.

The boundary is when $Z_{0T}^2 = 0$:

$$
-\frac{\omega_c^2 L^2}{4} + \frac{L}{C} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\omega_c^2 L^2}{4} = \frac{L}{C}
$$

$$
\omega_c^2 = \frac{4}{LC}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}}
$$

**Logic of each step:**
1. Substitute the actual reactance expressions.
2. Recognize $Z_{0T}^2$ loses real-positive character beyond $\omega_c$.
3. Set $Z_{0T}^2 = 0$ to find the boundary frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form for π-section:** The same procedure gives $\omega_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$, confirming that T and π prototype sections have identical cut-offs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Design Equations for Prototype LPF (Source/Load Resistance $R_0$)

At $\omega = 0$, the inductor is a short and capacitor is an open. The network reduces to a pure resistance equal to $R_0$.

For **T-section prototype LPF** at DC:

$$
Z_{0T}\Big|_{\omega=0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = R_0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = R_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{L}{C} = R_0^2
$$

Combined with $\omega_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$, solving:

From $L = R_0^2 C$:

$$
\omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{R_0^2 C \cdot C}} = \frac{2}{R_0 C}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{2}{R_0 \omega_c}}
$$

And:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{2 R_0}{\omega_c}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the factor of 2:** For KTU questions, students often forget the factor of 2 when comparing against the standard textbook where the prototype is defined slightly differently. Use this carefully.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Frequency Transformation — LPF to HPF

A standard LPF prototype has transfer function $H_{LP}(s)$. To obtain HPF, we substitute:

$$
s \rightarrow \frac{\omega_c}{s}
$$

**Why does this work?** This transformation maps:
- $s = 0$ (DC of LPF) → $s \to \infty$ in HPF (capacitor blocks DC, lets high freq pass).
- $s = j\omega_c$ (cut-off of LPF) → $\omega_c / \omega_c = 1$, preserved as cut-off.

**Element transformation:**

For an inductor $L$ in the LPF prototype:

$$
Z_L(s) = sL \xrightarrow{s \to \omega_c/s} \frac{\omega_c L}{s} = \frac{1}{s \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_c L}}
$$

This is the impedance of a **capacitor** $C' = \dfrac{1}{\omega_c L}$.

For a capacitor $C$ in the LPF prototype:

$$
Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} \xrightarrow{s \to \omega_c/s} \frac{s}{\omega_c C} = s \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_c C}
$$

This is the impedance of an **inductor** $L' = \dfrac{1}{\omega_c C}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In going from LPF → HPF, inductors become capacitors and capacitors become inductors, with values transformed by $1/(\omega_c \cdot \text{original value})$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** Design a prototype **T-section LPF** with $R_0 = 600\;\Omega$ and $f_c = 1\;kHz$.

**Step 1:** Compute angular cut-off:

$$
\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = 2\pi \times 1000 = 2000\pi \approx 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply design formulas:

$$
L = \frac{2 R_0}{\omega_c} = \frac{2 \times 600}{6283.19} = \frac{1200}{6283.19}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 0.1910 \text{ H}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{2}{R_0 \omega_c} = \frac{2}{600 \times 6283.19} = \frac{2}{3.7699 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 0.5305\;\mu\text{F}}
$$

**Step 3:** Build the T-network:
- Series arm 1: $L/2 = 0.0955$ H
- Shunt arm: $C = 0.5305\;\mu$F
- Series arm 2: $L/2 = 0.0955$ H

This is a complete, mark-fetching KTU solution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filter Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[FILTERS]
    rootA --> B1[By Frequency Response]
    rootA --> B2[By Construction]
    rootA --> B3[By Magnitude Response]

    B1 --> C1[LPF Low Pass]
    B1 --> C2[HPF High Pass]
    B1 --> C3[BPF Band Pass]
    B1 --> C4[BSF Band Stop]

    B2 --> D1[Passive LC]
    B2 --> D2[Active Op-Amp]
    B2 --> D3[Digital DSP]

    B3 --> E1[Butterworth]
    B3 --> E2[Chebyshev]
    B3 --> E3[Elliptic Cauer]
    B3 --> E4[Bessel Thompson]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Passive T-Section LPF

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcNode[Source Vs Zs] --> armA[Series Arm Z1 slash 2 = L slash 2]
    armA --> nodeMid[Junction Node]
    nodeMid --> armB[Shunt Arm Z2 = C]
    armB --> gndA[Ground]
    nodeMid --> armC[Series Arm Z1 slash 2 = L slash 2]
    armC --> loadNode[Load RL]
    loadNode --> gndB[Ground]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Filter Design Procedure (Image Parameter Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1 Identify Specifications R0 fc order n]
    stepA --> stepB[Step 2 Choose Filter Type LPF HPF BPF BSF]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Apply Frequency Transformation to Get Prototype]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Compute L and C Using Prototype Formulas]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Assemble T or Pi Section Network]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Verify Cut off and Characteristic Impedance]
    stepF --> stepG[Design Complete]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Prototype T-Section LPF

```
       L/2             L/2
   o---MMM---+---MMM---o
             |
             |
             ===  C
             |
             |
            GND
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- Two series inductors, each of value $L/2$.
- A shunt capacitor $C$ connected to ground between them.

## 4.5 ASCII Schematic — Prototype π-Section LPF

```
       L
   o---MMM---+---o
             |
             |
            ===  2C
             |
            GND
             |
   o---------+---o
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a filter, ALWAYS label the element values explicitly (e.g., $L/2$, $2C$) and indicate the input/output ports with arrowheads. Examiners reward clarity.

## 4.6 Magnitude Response Curves — Conceptual ASCII Plot

```
LPF:                                HPF:
|H|                                 |H|
1|........                         1|        ........
 |        :                          |      :
 |        :                          |    :
 |        :                          |  :
 0|________:_____                  0|_:________
         ωc    ω                              ωc   ω

BPF:                                BSF:
|H|                                 |H|
1|   . . . .                       1|.........      
 |  :       :                        |  :     :
 |  :       :                        |  :     :
 0|__:_____:__                     0|__:__:__:___
       ω1 ω2 ω                          ω1 ω2  ω
```

- **LPF:** Flat → falls at $\omega_c$.
- **HPF:** Rises at $\omega_c$ → flat.
- **BPF:** Peak between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$.
- **BSF:** Notch (dip) between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each Part A question in KTU University Exams carries 3 marks and tests direct conceptual recall.**

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define a filter. List the four major classifications of filters based on frequency response."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A filter is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to transmit signals within a desired frequency range (passband) and attenuate signals outside this range (stopband).

**Four classifications based on frequency response:**
1. **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** — passes $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$.
2. **High Pass Filter (HPF)** — passes $\omega \ge \omega_c$.
3. **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** — passes $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$.
4. **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** — rejects $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**"Differentiate between Butterworth and Chebyshev filter responses."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Butterworth | Chebyshev |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| Passband | Maximally flat, no ripple | Equiripple (oscillations) |
| Stopband | Monotonic (smoothly falling) | Monotonic (Type I), equiripple (Type II) |
| Transition | Slower roll-off | Sharper roll-off for same order |
| Equation | $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$ | $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 T_n^2(\omega/\omega_c)}$ |
| Best for | General purpose requiring flat response | Applications needing sharp selectivity |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, Part B Module 4 questions are usually 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose ONE of the two alternatives.**

### Question B1 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Design a prototype T-section Low Pass Filter having:**
- Source/Load resistance $R_0 = 600\;\Omega$
- Cut-off frequency $f_c = 2\;kHz$

**(a)** Determine the values of L and C. [7 marks]
**(b)** Draw the circuit and determine the characteristic impedance $Z_{0T}$ at $\omega = 0$ and at $\omega = \omega_c$. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Design of L and C [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\omega_c$:

$$
\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = 2\pi \times 2000 = 4000\pi \approx 12566.37 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply prototype LPF formulas:

$$
L = \frac{2 R_0}{\omega_c} = \frac{2 \times 600}{12566.37} = \frac{1200}{12566.37}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 95.49 \text{ mH}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{2}{R_0 \omega_c} = \frac{2}{600 \times 12566.37}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 0.2653\;\mu\text{F}}
$$

**Each series arm** = $L/2 \approx 47.75$ mH; **Shunt arm** = $C \approx 0.2653\;\mu$F.

**Part (b) — Characteristic Impedance [7 marks]:**

**At $\omega = 0$ (DC condition):**

Inductor → short, capacitor → open. The T-network becomes a direct connection from input to output through the (now zero) inductors. The shunt branch (open capacitor) has infinite impedance.

$$
Z_{0T}\Big|_{\omega=0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{95.49 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2653 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
Z_{0T}\Big|_{\omega=0} = \sqrt{3.599 \times 10^{5}} \approx 600\;\Omega \;\checkmark
$$

This equals $R_0$ as required by the design constraint.

**At $\omega = \omega_c$:**

Substitute $\omega_c = 2/\sqrt{LC}$ into $Z_{0T}^2 = \dfrac{L}{C} - \dfrac{\omega^2 L^2}{4}$.

The expression $\dfrac{L}{C} - \dfrac{\omega_c^2 L^2}{4} = \dfrac{L}{C} - \dfrac{4 L^2}{4LC} = \dfrac{L}{C} - \dfrac{L}{C} = 0$.

$$
\boxed{Z_{0T}\Big|_{\omega=\omega_c} = 0\;\Omega}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** At cut-off, the characteristic impedance vanishes — energy is fully reflected; the filter transitions to the stopband.

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**For a prototype π-section Low Pass Filter with $R_0 = 50\;\Omega$ and $f_c = 5\;kHz$:**

**(a)** Determine L and C using frequency scaling. [7 marks]
**(b)** Sketch the magnitude response of an ideal and practical LPF, marking the passband, stopband, and cut-off frequency. Comment on roll-off. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Element Design [7 marks]:**

For a **prototype π-section LPF**, the design equations are (analogous to T-section but with halved $L$ and doubled $C$ per arm):

$$
L_\pi = \frac{R_0}{\omega_c}, \quad C_\pi = \frac{2}{R_0 \omega_c}
$$

**Step 1:** $\omega_c = 2\pi \times 5000 = 10000\pi \approx 31415.93$ rad/s.

**Step 2:**

$$
L_\pi = \frac{50}{31415.93} \approx 1.5915 \text{ mH}
$$

$$
C_\pi = \frac{2}{50 \times 31415.93} \approx 1.2732\;\mu\text{F}
$$

**Step 3:** Build the π-section:
- Series arm: $L_\pi \approx 1.59$ mH
- Each shunt arm: $C_\pi / 2 \approx 0.6366\;\mu$F (since π uses two shunt capacitors of value $C_\pi/2$ each).

**Verification at DC:**

$$
Z_{0\pi}\Big|_{\omega=0} = \sqrt{\frac{L_\pi}{C_\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5915 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2732 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36\;\Omega
$$

(Note: $Z_{0\pi}$ at DC is $R_0/\sqrt{2}$ for the π-section, a known difference from T.)

**Part (b) — Magnitude Response Sketch [7 marks]:**

**Ideal LPF Response:**

|H(jω)|
   1 |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|
     |         |
   0 |_________|________
            ωc          ω

- Perfect transmission for $\omega \le \omega_c$.
- Total rejection for $\omega > \omega_c$.
- Vertical (infinite slope) transition.

**Practical (1st-order) LPF Response:**

|H(jω)|
   1 |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
     |         \
     |          \  (slope = -20 dB/decade)
     |           \
     |            \
   0 |_____________\______
            ωc           ω

- Gradual transition.
- **Roll-off rate:** $-20$ dB/decade for 1st-order, $-40$ dB/decade for 2nd-order, etc.
- Always has a transition band between passband and stopband.

**Comment on Roll-off:**

Roll-off is determined by filter **order** $n$:
- 1st-order LPF: $-20$ dB/decade
- 2nd-order LPF: $-40$ dB/decade
- $n^{th}$-order LPF: $-20n$ dB/decade

> [!NOTE]
> Higher-order filters give sharper transitions but require more components (more L's and C's) and introduce more phase distortion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Factor-of-2 Error:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in $L = 2R_0/\omega_c$ vs $L = R_0/\omega_c$. Always re-derive before writing.
>
> 2. **Confusing T and π sections:** The element values **differ** between T and π prototypes. T uses $L/2$ in series, π uses $L$ in series. Examiners WILL check this.
>
> 3. **Wrong Units for $\omega_c$:** Using $f_c$ (Hz) instead of $\omega_c$ (rad/s) gives answers off by a factor of $2\pi$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Cut-off Equation:** $Z_{0T}$ at $\omega = \omega_c$ is **zero** — students often compute it incorrectly as $R_0$.
>
> 5. **Not Labeling the Schematic:** Drawings without labeled ports, element values, or arrowheads lose easy marks.
>
> 6. **Skipping Steps in Derivation:** Each algebraic step is worth partial marks. Even if you make a numerical error, showing all steps salvages 5/7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Filter Classification**
>
> ☐ A **filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network with a defined passband and stopband.
>
> ☐ Four primary types by frequency response: **LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF**.
>
> ☐ **LPF** passes low frequencies; uses series $L$ and shunt $C$.
>
> ☐ **HPF** passes high frequencies; uses series $C$ and shunt $L$.
>
> ☐ **BPF** passes a specific band; **Bandwidth** = $\omega_2 - \omega_1$, **Q** = $\omega_0 / BW$.
>
> ☐ **BSF (Notch)** rejects a specific band; commonly used to remove 50 Hz line interference.
>
> ☐ **Cut-off frequency** $\omega_c$ is defined at the **-3 dB point** ($\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$).
>
> ☐ **Roll-off** = $20n$ dB/decade where $n$ is filter order.
>
> ☐ **Butterworth:** flat passband, slower transition.
>
> ☐ **Chebyshev:** ripple in passband, sharper transition.
>
> ☐ **Elliptic (Cauer):** ripple in both passband and stopband, sharpest transition.
>
> ☐ **Bessel (Thompson):** linear phase, best for pulse preservation.
>
> ☐ **T-section $Z_{0T}$:** $\sqrt{Z_1^2/4 + Z_1 Z_2}$.
>
> ☐ **π-section $Z_{0\pi}$:** $Z_1 Z_2 / Z_{0T}$.
>
> ☐ **Prototype LPF:** $\omega_c = 2/\sqrt{LC}$, $L/C = R_0^2$.
>
> ☐ **Design formulas (T-section LPF):** $L = 2R_0/\omega_c$, $C = 2/(R_0 \omega_c)$.
>
> ☐ **Frequency transformation (LPF → HPF):** Replace $s$ by $\omega_c/s$; inductors become capacitors and vice-versa.
>
> ☐ **At cut-off** ($\omega = \omega_c$): $Z_{0T} = 0$ for the T-section LPF, marking the boundary between passband and stopband.
>
> ☐ For KTU, ALWAYS show complete derivation steps with units; partial marks are awarded generously.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — CLASSIFICATION OF FILTERS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristic Impedance (Z₀)** of a transmission line is defined as the **intrinsic, frequency-dependent ratio of the incident voltage wave to the incident current wave** propagating along an infinitely long, uniform transmission line. It is the impedance that the line *presents* to any source connected to it, and it represents the ratio of complex voltage to complex current for a single forward-traveling wave.

Mathematically, for a distributed parameter transmission line modeled with primary constants $R$ (resistance per unit length, $\Omega$/m), $L$ (inductance per unit length, H/m), $G$ (conductance per unit length, S/m), and $C$ (capacitance per unit length, F/m), the characteristic impedance is given by:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} \;\; \Omega$$

For the most common engineering scenario — the **lossless transmission line** ($R = 0$, $G = 0$) — this simplifies dramatically to a purely real, frequency-independent quantity:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;\; \Omega$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, frictionless conveyor belt with evenly spaced springs (representing $L$) and dampers/friction pads (representing $C$) attached along its length. If you push on one end, a wave of motion travels down the belt. The "characteristic impedance" is the **inherent push-back that the belt offers per unit speed of the wave**, determined entirely by the geometry of the belt — not by how far or how long you push.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** $Z_0$ is a *property of the line itself*, determined by its physical construction (geometry and material). It does **not** depend on the line length, the source, or the load. It is, in essence, the line's "natural electrical footprint."

A more relatable analogy: Think of $Z_0$ as the **"acoustic impedance"** of a long tube. When you blow air into it, the tube resists the airflow in a specific way dictated by its cross-section and length-to-diameter ratio — that's its characteristic impedance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$**: measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**
- **Standard values in practice**:
  - Coaxial cable (RG-58): **50 $\Omega$**
  - Coaxial cable (RG-59): **75 $\Omega$**
  - Twisted pair (Ethernet): **100 $\Omega$**
  - Parallel-wire TV antenna lead: **300 $\Omega$**

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to derive $Z_0$ from the telegrapher's equations.
> - Distinguish clearly between **lossless**, **distortionless**, and **low-loss** approximations.
> - Recognize $Z_0$ as the impedance seen looking into an *infinitely long* line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Characteristic Impedance

Characteristic impedance arises naturally when we solve the **telegrapher's equations** for a uniform transmission line. Consider an infinitesimal segment $dx$ of the line:

- Series impedance per unit length: $Z = R + j\omega L$
- Shunt admittance per unit length: $Y = G + j\omega C$

Applying KVL and KCL to this segment yields:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L)\, I = -Z\, I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C)\, V = -Y\, V$$

Differentiating the first equation with respect to $x$ and substituting the second:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -Z \frac{dI}{dx} = -Z(-YV) = ZY\, V$$

Similarly:

$$\frac{d^2 I}{dx^2} = YZ\, I$$

## 2.2 The Propagation Constant

The product $ZY = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)$ is the **propagation constant**, denoted $\gamma$:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$$

where $\alpha$ = attenuation constant (Np/m) and $\beta$ = phase constant (rad/m).

## 2.3 The General Solution & Birth of $Z_0$

The general solution for voltage is:

$$V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + V^- e^{+\gamma x}$$

Substituting back into the telegrapher's equation:

$$I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma x}$$

The coefficient relating voltage to current for a *single traveling wave* is, by definition, the **characteristic impedance**:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}}$$

## 2.4 Structural Breakdown of Cases

- **Lossless line ($R=0$, $G=0$)**: $\Rightarrow Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (purely real, frequency-independent)
- **Distortionless line ($R/L = G/C$)**: $\Rightarrow Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (still real and frequency-independent)
- **Low-loss line (small $R$, small $G$)**: $\Rightarrow Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C}$ with small resistive component
- **High-frequency approximation ($\omega L \gg R$, $\omega C \gg G$)**: $\Rightarrow Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C}$

## 2.5 Physical Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> $Z_0$ governs **signal integrity** in all high-frequency and communication systems. Any mismatch between $Z_0$ and the load impedance causes **reflections**, leading to standing waves, signal distortion, and potential damage to driving circuitry. This is why RF systems, Ethernet cables, and high-speed digital PCB traces are designed with precisely controlled impedances.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Case | Formula | Unit / Note |
|---|------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | General $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Lossless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$, purely real |
| 3 | Distortionless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$, independent of frequency |
| 4 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY}$ | Np/m + j(rad/m) |
| 5 | Voltage wave | $V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + V^- e^{+\gamma x}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Current wave | $I(x) = \dfrac{V^+}{Z_0}e^{-\gamma x} - \dfrac{V^-}{Z_0}e^{+\gamma x}$ | Amperes |
| 7 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Standing wave ratio | $\text{SWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Ratio $\geq 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: General Characteristic Impedance from Telegrapher's Equations

We begin with the distributed model of a transmission line. Consider the elementary section of length $\Delta x$:

### Step 1: Set up KVL around the series loop

$$V(x, t) - V(x + \Delta x, t) = R\,\Delta x \cdot I(x, t) + L\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}$$

### Step 2: Divide by $\Delta x$ and take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$

$$-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = R\,I + L\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

In the **phasor (steady-state sinusoidal)** domain, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \rightarrow j\omega$:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I = -Z\, I \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

### Step 3: Set up KCL at the shunt node

$$I(x, t) - I(x + \Delta x, t) = G\,\Delta x \cdot V(x + \Delta x, t) + C\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial V(x+\Delta x, t)}{\partial t}$$

### Step 4: Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$ and convert to phasor

$$-\frac{dI}{dx} = (G + j\omega C)\,V = Y\,V \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

### Step 5: Differentiate Eq. 1 with respect to $x$

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -Z\,\frac{dI}{dx}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Eq. 2 into the above

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -Z(-YV) = ZY\,V$$

### Step 7: Recognize the wave equation and define $\gamma^2 = ZY$

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} - \gamma^2 V = 0$$

### Step 8: Solve the second-order linear ODE

The general solution is a linear combination of two exponentials:

$$V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + V^- e^{+\gamma x}$$

### Step 9: Find the current by substituting into Eq. 1

$$I(x) = -\frac{1}{Z}\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{1}{Z}\left[-\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + \gamma V^- e^{+\gamma x}\right]$$

$$I(x) = \frac{\gamma}{Z}\,V^+ e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{\gamma}{Z}\,V^- e^{+\gamma x}$$

### Step 10: Define $Z_0 = Z/\gamma = \sqrt{Z/Y}$

Substituting $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY}$:

$$Z_0 = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{ZY}} = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}}$$

The current expression simplifies beautifully to:

$$I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of Step 10:** The forward wave $V^+$ produces a current $V^+/Z_0$ in the same direction, while the reflected wave $V^-$ produces a current $-V^-/Z_0$. The minus sign reveals that a reflected wave's current reverses relative to its voltage, which is consistent with energy flowing *backward* on the line.

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Lossless Line Specialization

Setting $R = 0$ and $G = 0$ in the general formula:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega L}{\omega C}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{j}{j}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

Notice that the $j\omega$ factors cancel *exactly*, eliminating all frequency dependence:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad \text{(Lossless)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Distortionless Line Condition

For a general line, $Z_0$ is complex and frequency-dependent:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{R(1 + j\omega L/R)}{G(1 + j\omega C/G)}}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1 + j\omega L/R}{1 + j\omega C/G}}$$

For $Z_0$ to be **frequency-independent (purely real)**, the two ratios inside the second square root must be equal:

$$\frac{L}{R} = \frac{C}{G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R}{L} = \frac{G}{C}$$

This is the **distortionless condition**. When satisfied:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

This is a classic result and explains why telephone and submarine cables are often loaded to satisfy this condition.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A transmission line has $R = 2\;\Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 2\;\mu\text{H/km}$, $G = 0.5\;\mu\text{S/km}$, $C = 5\;\text{nF/km}$. Compute $Z_0$ and $\gamma$ at $\omega = 10^6$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

$$Z = R + j\omega L = 2 + j(10^6)(2 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 + j2 \;\; \Omega/\text{km}$$

$$Y = G + j\omega C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} + j(10^6)(5 \times 10^{-9}) = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} + j\,5 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{S/km}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 + j2}{0.5 \times 10^{-6} + j\,5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

Computing magnitudes:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$$

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{(0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 + (5 \times 10^{-3})^2} \approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\vert Z_0 \vert \approx \sqrt{\frac{2.828}{0.005}} = \sqrt{565.6} \approx 23.78 \;\; \Omega$$

Phase of $Z$: $\theta_Z = \tan^{-1}(2/2) = 45°$
Phase of $Y$: $\theta_Y \approx \tan^{-1}(5 \times 10^{-3} / 0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \approx \tan^{-1}(10000) \approx 89.99°$

Phase of $Z_0$ = $(\theta_Z - \theta_Y)/2 \approx (45° - 90°)/2 \approx -22.5°$

$$\boxed{Z_0 \approx 23.78\,\angle\,{-22.5°} \;\; \Omega}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distributed Parameter Model of a Transmission Line

The following block diagram shows how the lumped-element distributed model is constructed, with each infinitesimal segment $\Delta x$ modeled as a series impedance and shunt admittance. The characteristic impedance emerges as a *global property* derived from the chain of these local segments.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSrc[Voltage Source V_s] --> nodeA
    nodeA[Series R.dx] --> nodeB[Series L.dx]
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC[Shunt G.dx to ground] --> nodeGND1[(Ground)]
    nodeC[Shunt C.dx to ground] --> nodeGND2[(Ground)]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Series R.dx next segment]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Series L.dx next segment]
    nodeE --> nodeF[...]
    nodeF --> nodeLoad[Load Z_L]
    nodeLoad --> nodeGND3[(Ground)]
```

## 4.2 Forward and Reflected Wave Decomposition — Functional Architecture

This flow block illustrates how the total line voltage and current are decomposed into a forward (incident) wave and a backward (reflected) wave. The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ acts as the *scaling bridge* between the voltage and current of each wave component.

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[Total Voltage V x] --> splitA{Decompose by direction}
    splitA --> fwdV[V+ e to power minus gamma x]
    splitA --> refV[V- e to power plus gamma x]
    srcB[Total Current I x] --> splitB{Divide by Z_0}
    fwdV --> divA[Divide by Z_0]
    refV --> divB[Subtract and divide by Z_0]
    divA --> fwdI[Forward current I forward]
    divB --> refI[Reflected current I reverse]
    fwdI --> sumI[Combine as traveling waves]
    refI --> sumI
    sumI --> out[Net signal at any point x]
```

## 4.3 Special-Case Decision Tree for $Z_0$

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given R L G C of line] --> q1{Is R equal to 0 AND G equal to 0}
    q1 -- Yes --> case1[Use Z_0 equals sqrt L over C]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Does R over L equal G over C}
    q2 -- Yes --> case2[Distortionless case: Z_0 equals sqrt L over C]
    q2 -- No --> q3{High frequency omega L much greater than R}
    q3 -- Yes --> case3[Low-loss approx: Z_0 approx sqrt L over C]
    q3 -- No --> case4[Full general formula: Z_0 equals sqrt R plus j omega L over G plus j omega C]
```

## 4.4 Phasor Relationship Between V and I

This block-level representation captures the phasor relation between the incident voltage and current waves. Each wave independently sees the impedance $Z_0$, but the reflected wave's current is inverted because energy flows back toward the source.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Incident Wave
        vi[V+ phasor] --> ri[Ratio of voltage to current]
        ri --> z0a[Z_0 = sqrt Z over Y]
        z0a --> ii[I+ = V+ over Z_0]
    end
    subgraph Reflected Wave
        vr[V- phasor] --> rr[Ratio with sign reversal]
        rr --> z0b[Z_0 same value]
        z0b --> ir[I- = minus V- over Z_0]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. Why is it independent of line length?

**Model Answer:**

Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is the intrinsic impedance of a transmission line, defined as the ratio of the complex amplitudes of the voltage and current waves of a single forward-propagating wave on the line:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

It is independent of line length because $Z_0$ depends only on the **per-unit-length parameters** ($R$, $L$, $G$, $C$) which are themselves determined by the line's geometry and material — not by how many segments are cascaded. The line acts as an *infinite chain of identical sections*, and the impedance presented at any point is the same as that of an infinitely long line.

### Question 2 (Numerical / Formula Recall)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.5\;\mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 50\;\text{pF/m}$. Calculate its characteristic impedance.

**Model Answer:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{10^{4}} = 100 \;\; \Omega$$

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a transmission line from the telegrapher's equations. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a lossy transmission line with $R = 1\;\Omega/\text{km}$, $L = 0.5\;\text{mH/km}$, $G = 5\;\mu\text{S/km}$, and $C = 0.1\;\mu\text{F/km}$, compute the characteristic impedance at $\omega = 5000$ rad/s. Comment on whether the line qualifies as a distortionless line. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from KVL on an elemental segment:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L) I$$

KCL gives:

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C) V$$

Differentiating the first and substituting:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)\, V = \gamma^2 V$$

The general solution is $V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + V^- e^{+\gamma x}$. Substituting back:

$$I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma x}, \quad \text{where } Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

**Assumptions:** line is uniform, linear, time-invariant, and the analysis is in the sinusoidal steady state (phasor domain).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Compute $\omega L$ and $\omega C$:

$$\omega L = 5000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \;\; \Omega/\text{km}$$

$$\omega C = 5000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{S/km}$$

$$Z = 1 + j2.5 \;\; \Omega/\text{km}$$

$$Y = 5 \times 10^{-6} + j5 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{S/km}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1 + j2.5}{5 \times 10^{-6} + j5 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

Magnitude: $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{1 + 6.25} = \sqrt{7.25} \approx 2.693$

$\vert Y \vert \approx \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 + (5 \times 10^{-4})^2} \approx 5 \times 10^{-4}$

$$\vert Z_0 \vert = \sqrt{\frac{2.693}{5 \times 10^{-4}}} \approx \sqrt{5386} \approx 73.4 \;\; \Omega$$

Phases: $\theta_Z = \tan^{-1}(2.5/1) = 68.2°$, $\theta_Y \approx 89.4°$

$$\theta_{Z_0} = \frac{68.2° - 89.4°}{2} = -10.6°$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 \approx 73.4\,\angle\,{-10.6°} \;\; \Omega}$$

**Distortionless test:** $R/L = 1/0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2000$; $G/C = 5 \times 10^{-6} / 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 50$.

Since $2000 \neq 50$, the line is **not distortionless**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of characteristic impedance. Discuss its behavior for lossless, distortionless, and low-loss lines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A distortionless line has $R = 10\;\Omega/\text{km}$ and $G = 40\;\mu\text{S/km}$. If $L = 5\;\text{mH/km}$, find $C$ and the characteristic impedance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Characteristic impedance represents the **inherent ratio of voltage to current** for an electromagnetic wave propagating along the line. Its physical significance:

1. **Signal Integrity**: It determines how much of the signal is reflected at the load. Impedance matching ($Z_L = Z_0$) eliminates reflections.
2. **Power Transfer**: Maximum power transfer to the load occurs when $Z_L = Z_0$.
3. **Wave Behavior**: For $Z_0$ purely real, no phase distortion exists between voltage and current of the incident wave.

**Behavior across cases:**
- **Lossless** ($R = 0$, $G = 0$): $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, purely real, independent of frequency.
- **Distortionless** ($R/L = G/C$): $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, still purely real and frequency-independent, but with attenuation present.
- **Low-loss** ($\omega L \gg R$, $\omega C \gg G$): $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C}$ with a small complex part.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

For a distortionless line, $R/L = G/C$:

$$\frac{10}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{C}$$

$$2000 = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{C}$$

$$C = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{2000} = 20 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{F/km} = 20 \;\; \text{nF/km}$$

Characteristic impedance:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{5}} \approx 500 \;\; \Omega$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = 500 \;\; \Omega, \quad C = 20 \;\; \text{nF/km}}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Students Should Avoid:**
> 1. **Sign error in current expression**: Many students write $I(x) = \dfrac{V^+}{Z_0}e^{-\gamma x} + \dfrac{V^-}{Z_0}e^{+\gamma x}$ — the **reflected current term must carry a negative sign** because reflected energy flows backward.
> 2. **Forgetting phasor domain**: The derivative $\partial/\partial t$ in the time-domain telegrapher's equations must be converted to $j\omega$ in the phasor domain — omitting this step leads to a non-phasor $Z_0$ expression.
> 3. **Confusing lossless with distortionless**: A lossless line has $R = G = 0$. A distortionless line may still have non-zero $R$ and $G$ provided their ratios match $L/C$. Both yield $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.
> 4. **Units mismatch**: Always carry the units of $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ **per unit length**. The $Z_0$ result is in Ohms, *not* Ohms per meter.
> 5. **Magnitude vs complex form**: Examiners expect $Z_0$ in **complex (polar or rectangular) form** when the line is lossy, not just magnitude.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: $Z_0$ is the ratio $V^+/I^+$ for a forward wave; it equals $\sqrt{Z/Y}$.
- **General formula**: $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)/(G + j\omega C)}$.
- **Lossless simplification**: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — purely real, frequency-independent.
- **Distortionless condition**: $R/L = G/C$ ensures $Z_0$ is real and frequency-independent.
- **Low-loss approximation**: At high $\omega$, $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C}$ even when $R, G \neq 0$.
- **Physical meaning**: $Z_0$ is the line's *intrinsic* impedance — independent of length, source, or load.
- **Practical values**: 50 $\Omega$, 75 $\Omega$, 100 $\Omega$, 300 $\Omega$ are standard.
- **Engineering relevance**: Critical for impedance matching, reflection elimination, and signal integrity in RF and high-speed digital systems.
- **Exam tip**: Always start from telegrapher's equations, derive the wave equation, propose a solution, and identify $Z_0$ as the coupling coefficient between $V$ and $I$.
- **Pitfall to remember**: Reflected current term carries a **negative sign**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Constant K Low Pass Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Constant K Low Pass Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **Constant-K Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a fundamental, classical filter network constructed using the **Image Parameter Method**, in which the product of the series arm impedance ($Z_1$) and the shunt arm impedance ($Z_2$) is a **real, positive, frequency-independent constant**, denoted as $K^2$. This constant is defined as:

$$K^2 = Z_1 \cdot Z_2 = \frac{L}{C} \quad \text{(constant, real, positive)}$$

For a **Low Pass Filter** topology, the configuration is a **reactance network** where:

- The **series arm** contains a pure **inductor** $Z_1 = j\omega L$
- The **shunt arm** contains a pure **capacitor** $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

The name *Constant-K* literally arises because $Z_1 Z_2 = K^2$ holds true for **all frequencies** $\omega$, making $K$ a true design constant chosen at the start of the design procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Constant-K filter is the *foundational prototype* from which all advanced filters (m-derived, composite) are derived. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for both KTU ESE (End Semester) and internal module tests.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at the Club" Model

Imagine an **exclusive nightclub** with two bouncers working together at the entrance:

1. **The Slow-Motion Bouncer (Inductor $L$):** Only allows people who move *slowly* (i.e., low-frequency, slowly changing signals). When someone tries to rush in too fast (high-frequency), the inductor creates a strong *back-EMF* that physically resists them. Mathematically: $X_L = \omega L$, so as $\omega \uparrow$, opposition $\uparrow$.

2. **The Side-Street Drain (Capacitor $C$):** Any fast-mover (high-frequency signal) that sneaks past the inductor gets immediately diverted into a side alley (shunted to ground) by the capacitor. Mathematically: $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$, so as $\omega \uparrow$, shunting effectiveness $\uparrow$.

**Result:** Slow, low-frequency signals **pass through cleanly**, while fast, high-frequency signals are **doubly-stopped** — first by the inductor's resistance, then by the capacitor's short-circuit shunting to ground. This is the operational essence of every Low Pass Filter.

| Bouncer Analogy | Circuit Equivalent | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Slow-motion bouncer | Inductor $L$ | Blocks high frequencies ($X_L \propto \omega$) |
| Side-street drain | Capacitor $C$ | Shunts high frequencies to ground ($X_C \propto 1/\omega$) |
| VIP pass | Signal in pass-band | $0 \le f \le f_c$, attenuation $= 0$ |
| Banned list | Stop-band ($f > f_c$) | Signals severely attenuated |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Design Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Fundamental Design Parameters** (must be specified by the user/designer):
>
> 1. **Cutoff Frequency** $f_c$ — the boundary frequency separating pass-band from stop-band (in **Hz** or **rad/sec**).
> 2. **Design (Nominal) Impedance** $R_0$ — the characteristic impedance the filter must present at $f = 0$ (in **$\Omega$**). Mathematically, $R_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.

These two values **completely determine** the filter. Once known, $L$ and $C$ are uniquely calculated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Why and How

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Establish the K-Constraint:**
The defining property of any Constant-K filter is the impedance product relation:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_2 = (j\omega L) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = \frac{L}{C} = K^2$$

Notice how $\omega$ (frequency) **cancels out completely**. This is what makes $K$ a *constant* — it survives every frequency. This is the structural reason these filters carry the "Constant-K" label.

**Step 2 — Define the Cutoff Frequency:**
The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is where the **pass-band ends and the stop-band begins**. Mathematically, it is the frequency at which the half-section's **iterative (image) impedance** becomes purely real and matches the design resistance $R_0$. This yields:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic (Image) Impedance:**
For the two fundamental sections:

$$Z_{0T} = \sqrt{Z_1 Z_2} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}$$

$$Z_{0\pi} = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Propagation Constant & Attenuation:**
The propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ separates into:

- **Pass-band** ($f < f_c$): $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)$ — pure phase shift, zero loss.
- **Stop-band** ($f > f_c$): $\beta = 0$, $\alpha = \cosh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)$ — pure attenuation, zero phase.

**Step 5 — Why It Works in Pass-Band:**
At very low frequencies ($f \to 0$):
- $X_L = \omega L \to 0$ → Inductor is a *short-circuit* (zero opposition).
- $X_C = 1/(\omega C) \to \infty$ → Capacitor is an *open-circuit* (infinite opposition, no shunting).

So the signal passes straight through with **zero attenuation**.

**Step 6 — Why It Attenuates in Stop-Band:**
At very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$):
- $X_L = \omega L \to \infty$ → Inductor is an *open-circuit* (blocks signal).
- $X_C = 1/(\omega C) \to 0$ → Capacitor is a *short-circuit* to ground (diverts signal).

So the signal is **doubly blocked** — by the inductor at the input, and by the capacitor draining whatever gets through.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU ESE.** Every question on Constant-K LPF design reduces to a clever rearrangement of the formulas below.

| # | Formula | Description | Valid In |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K^2 = Z_1 Z_2 = L/C = R_0^2$ | Constant-K defining relation | All frequencies |
| 2 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Cutoff frequency (Hz) | Design stage |
| 3 | $\omega_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Cutoff frequency (rad/s) | Design stage |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{R_0}{\pi f_c}$ | Series inductance (Henry) | T or $\pi$ full-section |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{1}{\pi f_c R_0}$ | Shunt capacitance (Farad) | T or $\pi$ full-section |
| 6 | $Z_{0T} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}$ | Characteristic impedance, T-section | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 7 | $Z_{0\pi} = \dfrac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}}$ | Characteristic impedance, $\pi$-section | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$ | Attenuation constant (Nepers) | Stop-band ($f > f_c$) |
| 9 | $\beta = \cos^{-1}(f/f_c)$ | Phase constant (radians) | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 10 | $\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(e^\alpha) = 8.686 \alpha$ | Attenuation in dB | Stop-band |
| 11 | $Z_{1T} = L/2$, $Z_{2T} = C$ | Half T-section components | T-half |
| 12 | $Z_{1\pi} = L$, $Z_{2\pi} = 2C$ | Half $\pi$-section components | $\pi$-half |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition reminder:** At exactly $f = f_c$, the term $\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2} \to 0$, which means $Z_{0T} \to 0$ and $Z_{0\pi} \to \infty$. This is an *impedance mismatch* — the source and load see very different impedances, and reflections occur at this boundary. This is one of the key motivations for the **m-derived filter** (a higher-order extension covered later in the module).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Constant-K LPFs are the workhorse filters of analog electronics and have direct applications in:

- **Audio Engineering:** Removing high-frequency hiss/noise from audio signals (crossover networks in loudspeakers).
- **Power Electronics:** Smoothing rectified DC outputs by removing high-frequency switching ripple.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel filtering in analog telephone and early radio systems.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Anti-aliasing filters placed before analog-to-digital converters (ADCs).
- **Medical Electronics (ECG/EEG machines):** Filtering out 50/60 Hz line-noise interference and EMG high-frequency noise.

The **design procedure** is fundamentally a **two-equation, two-unknown** problem: given $f_c$ and $R_0$, solve simultaneously for $L$ and $C$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Cutoff Frequency $f_c$

**Starting point:** The image (iterative) impedance of a T-section is given by the classical image-parameter formula:

$$Z_{0T} = \sqrt{Z_1^2 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{2} + \sqrt{\frac{Z_2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2}$$

But for the simpler symmetrical network analysis, we use the **characteristic impedance** of an LC ladder:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_1 Z_2 + \frac{Z_1^2}{4}}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $Z_1 = j\omega L$ and $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{(j\omega L)\left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + \frac{(j\omega L)^2}{4}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the first term. Note that $(j\omega L)\left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = \dfrac{L}{C} = K^2$ (frequency-independent constant):

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C} - \frac{\omega^2 L^2}{4}}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\dfrac{L}{C}$ strategically:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}\left(1 - \frac{\omega^2 L C}{4}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{LC}{4}$ has units of $\dfrac{1}{\omega^2}$. Define the cutoff:

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{4}{LC} \implies \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\dfrac{L}{C} = R_0^2$. Therefore:

$$Z_0 = R_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Hz form using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}}}$$

The cutoff frequency is precisely where $\omega = \omega_c$, making $Z_0 = 0$ (purely real, zero impedance — short-circuit condition for T-section, open-circuit for $\pi$-section).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Equations for $L$ and $C$

We now have two coupled equations from the analysis above:

$$\text{(i)} \quad \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{(ii)} \quad R_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 1:** From (ii), solve for $L$:

$$L = R_0^2 \, C$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into (i):

$$\omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{R_0^2 \, C \cdot C}} = \frac{2}{R_0 \, C}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $C$:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{2}{\omega_c R_0} = \frac{1}{\pi f_c R_0}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into $L = R_0^2 \, C$:

$$L = R_0^2 \cdot \frac{2}{\omega_c R_0} = \frac{2 R_0}{\omega_c}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{R_0}{\pi f_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check (dimensional analysis):**
> - $L$ has units of $\Omega / (\text{rad/s}) = \Omega \cdot \text{s} = \text{Henry}$. ✓
> - $C$ has units of $1 / (\Omega \cdot \text{rad/s}) = \text{s}/\Omega = \text{Farad}$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Attenuation in Stop-Band

**Step 1:** The propagation constant is defined as:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{Z_1}{2 Z_2} + \sqrt{\frac{Z_1^2}{4 Z_2^2} + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Z_1 = j\omega L$, $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C)$:

$$\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = (j\omega L)(j\omega C) = -\omega^2 LC$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\gamma = \cosh^{-1}\left(-\frac{\omega^2 LC}{2} + \sqrt{\frac{\omega^4 L^2 C^2}{4} - \omega^2 LC}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\omega^2 LC$ inside the square root:

$$\gamma = \cosh^{-1}\left(-\frac{\omega^2 LC}{2} + \omega\sqrt{LC}\sqrt{\frac{\omega^2 LC}{4} - 1}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Recognize $\omega^2 LC = (\omega/\omega_c)^2 \cdot 4$. After algebra, the clean result is:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j0 = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \omega > \omega_c$$

Therefore, in the **stop-band**:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right) \quad \text{Nepers}}$$

Converting to decibels (more common in practice):

$$\boxed{\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right) \quad \text{dB}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Design Problem)

> **Problem:** Design a Constant-K Low Pass Filter (both T and $\pi$ sections) with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2 \text{ kHz}$ and design resistance $R_0 = 600 \text{ }\Omega$.

### Solution — Full Sections

**Step 1:** Apply the design formulas:

$$L = \frac{R_0}{\pi f_c} = \frac{600}{\pi \times 2000} = \frac{600}{6283.19} \approx 95.49 \text{ mH}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{\pi f_c R_0} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 2000 \times 600} = \frac{1}{3.77 \times 10^6} \approx 0.265 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Solution — T-Section Half

**Step 2:** Split into halves. For a **T-section**, each half contains $L/2$ in series and $C$ in shunt:

$$L_T = \frac{L}{2} \approx 47.75 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_T = C \approx 0.265 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Solution — $\pi$-Section Half

**Step 3:** For a **$\pi$-section**, each half contains $L$ in series and $2C$ in shunt:

$$L_\pi = L \approx 95.49 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_\pi = 2C \approx 0.530 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Verification

**Step 4:** Verify the cutoff by plugging back:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{(95.49 \times 10^{-3})(0.265 \times 10^{-6})}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{2.530 \times 10^{-8}}} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 1.591 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2000 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners give full marks only when you write **all three steps**: design formula → substitution → numerical answer with correct **units**. A bare numerical answer without units loses 1 mark.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Attenuation Calculation

> **Problem:** For the filter designed above ($f_c = 2$ kHz, $R_0 = 600 \text{ }\Omega$), find the attenuation at $f = 5 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the frequency ratio:

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{5000}{2000} = 2.5$$

**Step 2:** Since $f > f_c$, we are in the **stop-band**. Apply attenuation formula:

$$\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(2.5)$$

**Step 3:** Use identity $\cosh^{-1}(x) = \ln(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})$:

$$\alpha = \ln(2.5 + \sqrt{2.5^2 - 1}) = \ln(2.5 + \sqrt{5.25}) = \ln(2.5 + 2.291)$$

$$\alpha = \ln(4.791) \approx 1.566 \text{ Nepers}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:

$$\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \times 1.566 \approx 13.6 \text{ dB}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Topology — T and $\pi$ Full Sections

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T_SECTION["T SECTION FULL"]
        In1((Source)) --- L1A["L/2"]
        L1A --- NodeMidT(( ))
        NodeMidT --- L1B["L/2"]
        L1B --- Out1((Load))
        NodeMidT --- CT["C"]
        CT --- GND1((GND))
    end

    subgraph PI_SECTION["PI SECTION FULL"]
        In2((Source)) --- LP["L"]
        LP --- NodeOut2((Load))
        NodeOut2 --- Out2p(( ))
        CP1["2C"] --- NodeIn2
        NodeIn2 --- GND2((GND))
        CP2["2C"] --- NodeOut2
        Out2p --- GND3((GND))
    end
```

> **Reading the schematic:** In the T-section, the inductor $L$ is split into two halves ($L/2$ each) flanking a central shunt capacitor $C$. In the $\pi$-section, the capacitor $C$ is split into two halves ($2C$ each) flanking a central series inductor $L$.

---

## 4.2 Half-Section Prototype (The Building Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src((Vin)) --- Lh["L"]
    Lh --- Mid((Vout))
    Mid --- LoadR["R Load"]
    Mid --- Ch["C"]
    Ch --- Gnd((GND))
    style Lh fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Ch fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Half-section behavior:** This is the fundamental building block. A T-section half has $L$ in series and $C$ in shunt. Two such halves back-to-back form a full T-section. Two mirrored halves form a $\pi$-section.

---

## 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristics (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIGNAL SPECTRUM"]
        A["Signal with components at 100 Hz, 1 kHz, 10 kHz"]
    end

    subgraph FILTER["CONSTANT K LOW PASS FILTER"]
        B["Series Inductor L: blocks high f"]
        C["Shunt Capacitor C: drains high f to GND"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SPECTRUM"]
        D["100 Hz: passes unchanged"]
        E["1 kHz: passes near unchanged"]
        F["10 kHz: severely attenuated"]
    end

    A --> B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F

    style B fill:#ffd966
    style C fill:#a4c2f4
```

---

## 4.4 Attenuation vs. Frequency Curve (Block Description)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PAS["PASS BAND"]
        PB["Alpha = 0 dB, signal passes cleanly, 0 to fc"]
    end

    subgraph CUTO["CUTOFF POINT"]
        CP["f = fc, Alpha = 3 dB boundary, impedance mismatch"]
    end

    subgraph STOP["STOP BAND"]
        SB["Alpha increases monotonically as f grows beyond fc, grows logarithmically toward infinity"]
    end

    PAS --> CUTO --> STOP
    style PAS fill:#d9ead3
    style CUTO fill:#fce5cd
    style STOP fill:#f4cccc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block description of attenuation curve:** The plot has three distinct regions. (1) **Pass-band (flat plateau):** $\alpha = 0$ from $f = 0$ to $f = f_c$. (2) **Transition region:** Sharp corner at $f_c$ where attenuation begins. (3) **Stop-band (rising):** $\alpha$ grows monotonically, mathematically as $\cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$. The transition is *not infinitely sharp* — this is a known limitation of Constant-K filters and motivates m-derived designs.

---

## 4.5 Design Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Step1["Step 1: User specifies cutoff frequency fc"]
    Step2["Step 2: User specifies design resistance R0"]
    Step3["Step 3: Compute L using L = R0 divided by pi times fc"]
    Step4["Step 4: Compute C using C = 1 divided by pi times fc times R0"]
    Step5{"Step 5: Choose section type T or Pi"}
    Step6T["Step 6T: T-section uses L by 2 series, C shunt"]
    Step6P["Step 6P: Pi-section uses L series, 2C shunt"]
    Step7["Step 7: Verify cutoff using fc equals 1 over pi times square root of LC"]
    Step8["Step 8: Calculate attenuation at desired f"]

    Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6T
    Step5 --> Step6P
    Step6T --> Step7
    Step6P --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8
```

> **Reading the workflow:** The design follows a strictly linear pipeline: specification → computation → topology selection → verification → analysis. This sequence is what KTU examiners expect students to follow when answering 14-mark design problems.
